The emphasis is placed on investigation of solution accuracy by considered artificial neural systems for 13.5 nm and 11.2 nm wavelengths. Numerical experiments on realistic 2D and 3D masks demonstrate that PINNs and neural operators achieve competitive accuracy and significantly reduced prediction times, with the proposed WGNO architecture reaching state-of-the-art performance. The presented neural operator has pronounced generalizing properties, meaning that for unseen problem parameters it delivers a solution accuracy close to that for parameters seen in the training dataset. These results provide a highly efficient solution for accelerating the design and optimization workflows of next-generation lithography masks.
one_liner: A hybrid waveguide neural operator accelerates EUV mask diffraction simulation while retaining competitive accuracy on realistic 2D and 3D masks.
why_it_matters: Fast, accurate EUV diffraction solvers can shorten lithography mask design cycles and improve optimization for next-generation semiconductor fabrication.
detailed_summary: The paper benchmarks physics-informed neural networks and neural operators for solving EUV electromagnetic diffraction at 13.5 nm and 11.2 nm against modern numerical solvers and exact-solution test cases. Its main technical contribution is a Waveguide Neural Operator that replaces the most expensive components of a waveguide-method solver with a learned model. On realistic 2D and 3D lithography masks, the approach delivers competitive accuracy with much lower inference time and shows strong generalization to unseen mask parameters.
main_result: The proposed Waveguide Neural Operator achieves state-of-the-art EUV mask diffraction prediction with substantially reduced runtime and good out-of-distribution generalization.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to computational photonics in chip manufacturing, where faster forward models can accelerate mask design, verification, and process optimization.

title: Benchmarking quantum simulation with neutron-scattering experiments
abstract: A central goal of quantum computation is the realistic simulation of quantum materials. Although quantum processors have advanced rapidly in scale and fidelity, it has remained unclear whether pre-fault-tolerant devices can perform quantitatively reliable material simulations within their limited gate budgets. Here, we demonstrate that a superconducting quantum processor operating on up to 50 qubits can already produce meaningful, quantitative comparisons with inelastic neutron-scattering measurements of KCuF$_3$, a canonical realization of a gapless Luttinger liquid system with a strongly correlated ground state and a spectrum of emergent spinons. The quantum simulation is enabled by a quantum-classical workflow for computing dynamical structure factors (DSFs). The resulting spectra are benchmarked against experimental measurements using multiple metrics, highlighting the impact of circuit depth and circuit fidelity on simulation accuracy. Finally, we extend our simulations to 1D XXZ Heisenberg model with next-nearest neighbor interactions and a strong anisotropy, producing a gapped excitation spectrum, which could be used to describe the CsCoX$_3$ compounds above the Néel temperature. Our results establish a framework for computing DSFs for quantum materials in classically challenging regimes of strong entanglement and long-range interactions, enabling quantum simulations that are directly testable against laboratory measurements.
one_liner: A 50-qubit superconducting processor computes dynamical structure factors that quantitatively match neutron-scattering data for KCuF3.
why_it_matters: It shows pre-fault-tolerant hardware can already produce experimentally testable simulations of strongly correlated quantum materials.
detailed_summary: The authors implement a quantum-classical workflow for calculating dynamical structure factors on a superconducting quantum processor using up to 50 qubits. They benchmark the resulting spectra against inelastic neutron-scattering measurements of KCuF3, a gapless Luttinger liquid with emergent spinon excitations, and analyze how circuit depth and fidelity affect agreement. They further extend the method to a frustrated and anisotropic 1D XXZ Heisenberg model with next-nearest-neighbor couplings, obtaining a gapped excitation spectrum relevant to CsCoX3-type materials.
main_result: The processor produces dynamical structure factors that quantitatively agree with neutron-scattering experiments for a canonical strongly correlated material.
application_angle: The work connects quantum hardware outputs directly to laboratory observables, making near-term processors more useful for materials modeling and experimental validation.

title: Entanglement advantage in sensing power-law spatiotemporal noise correlations
abstract: Noise sensing underlies many physical applications including tests of non-classicality, thermometry, verification of correlated phases of quantum matter, and characterization of criticality. While previous works have shown that quantum resources such as entanglement and squeezing can enhance the sensitivity in estimating deterministic signals, less is known about the entanglement advantage in sensing correlated stochastic signals (noise). In this work, we compute the fundamental sensitivity limits of quantum sensors in probing spatiotemporally correlated noise. We first prove the fundamental quantum limits in sensing spatially correlated Markovian noise using entangled and unentangled sensors, respectively. Focusing on power-law spatial noise correlations, which naturally arise in condensed matter systems with long-range interactions and/or near criticality, we further derive a scalable entanglement advantage when the power-law decays slowly. Then, considering a target signal with a $1/f^{p}$-type spectrum, we demonstrate that non-Markovianity may entirely modify the nature of entanglement advantage in estimating spatial noise correlations. Our protocols can be implemented using state-of-the-art quantum sensing platforms including solid-state defects, superconducting circuits, and neutral atoms.
one_liner: The paper derives when entanglement gives scalable gains for estimating spatial and temporal noise correlations with quantum sensor arrays.
why_it_matters: It identifies concrete noise regimes where entangled probes outperform separable sensors for realistic sensing tasks tied to many-body physics and criticality.
detailed_summary: The work establishes fundamental sensitivity limits for estimating spatiotemporally correlated stochastic noise with entangled and unentangled quantum sensors. For spatially correlated Markovian noise with slowly decaying power-law correlations, it proves a scalable entanglement advantage in estimating correlation parameters. It also shows that for non-Markovian targets with 1/f^p spectra, the structure of the entanglement advantage can change qualitatively. The protocols are framed for implementation on solid-state defects, superconducting circuits, and neutral-atom platforms.
main_result: Entangled sensors can achieve scalable sensitivity improvements for power-law correlated noise, with the advantage strongly altered by non-Markovian temporal spectra.
application_angle: These results guide the design of practical quantum sensing protocols for materials characterization, thermometry, and probing correlated or critical environments.

title: Quantum Noise Suppression at Scale with Crosstalk-Robust Gate Sets
abstract: We introduce crosstalk-robust gate sets, which are obtained using a novel, scalable optimal control problem exploiting locality. Through the suppression of pairwise quantum crosstalk, the gate sets enable robustness that extends to multi-qubit circuits. The IBM Quantum Platform devices provide a testbed for our gate sets, where we study their efficacy via error suppression protocols and randomized parallel single-qubit circuits of up to eight qubits. Furthermore, we provide the first known assessment of the impact of complete optimal control gate sets on quantum algorithms. Using a Hamiltonian simulation of a four-qubit transverse field Ising model, we show that noise-informed gates enhance median algorithmic performance by a factor of four over baseline Gaussian gates using the same calibration procedures. Lastly, we provide numerical evidence that optimized gate sets enable larger qubit-qubit coupling strengths that can cut two-qubit gate times in half. This result confirms that hardware-software co-design using quantum optimal control can create new opportunities for quantum computing architectures.
one_liner: Crosstalk-robust optimal-control gate sets suppress noise on IBM devices and improve algorithm performance by 4x in a model simulation.
why_it_matters: Crosstalk is a major bottleneck in scaling processors, so gate sets that mitigate it without changing hardware can materially improve usable computation.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces scalable crosstalk-robust gate sets obtained by solving a locality-exploiting optimal-control problem that suppresses pairwise crosstalk while benefiting multi-qubit circuits. On IBM Quantum devices, the gates are tested with error-suppression protocols and randomized parallel single-qubit circuits involving up to eight qubits. In a four-qubit transverse-field Ising simulation, the noise-informed gates improve median algorithmic performance by a factor of four over baseline Gaussian gates using the same calibration flow. Numerical results also indicate stronger usable couplings and potential two-qubit gate times roughly half as long.
platform_or_system: superconducting qubits on IBM Quantum devices
main_result: Optimized crosstalk-robust gate sets reduce effective noise at scale and deliver a 4x median performance boost in a representative quantum algorithm.
application_angle: The approach is immediately relevant to improving current superconducting processors through control-stack upgrades rather than requiring new hardware.

title: A fault-tolerant encoding for qubit-controlled collective spins
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is indispensable for scalable quantum computing, but implementing it with minimal hardware overhead remains a central challenge. Large spin systems with collective degrees of freedom offer a promising route to reducing the control complexity of qubit architectures while retaining a large Hilbert space for fault-tolerant encoding. However, existing proposals for logical gates and QEC in spin ensembles generally rely on inefficient higher-order interactions. Here we introduce spin-N-Cat codes, which encode logical qubits in superpositions of spin-coherent states and generalize bosonic Cat codes to the modular subspaces of permutationally symmetric spin ensembles. The code corrects collective and individual dephasing, excitation, and decay errors. We also present an efficient physical realization in central-spin systems, such as a quantum dot, where encoding, decoding, and a universal, fault-tolerant, and bias-preserving gate set are implemented using only first-order interactions. Numerical simulations demonstrate high logical fidelity under dephasing and excitation-decay noise, independent of noise bias, and that full QEC cycles are feasible with realistic microscopic parameters. For the large collective spins available in quantum dots, this translates into a substantial extension of coherence time. Our results establish spin-N-Cat codes as a scalable, hardware-efficient approach to QEC in spin-based quantum architectures.
one_liner: Spin-N-Cat codes encode logical qubits in collective spins and enable fault-tolerant, bias-preserving gates using only first-order interactions.
why_it_matters: It offers a hardware-efficient path to quantum error correction in spin systems, reducing control complexity while extending coherence.
detailed_summary: The authors define spin-N-Cat codes as superpositions of spin-coherent states in modular subspaces of permutationally symmetric spin ensembles, extending bosonic cat-code ideas to collective-spin hardware. The code corrects collective and individual dephasing, excitation, and decay errors and supports encoding, decoding, and a universal bias-preserving logical gate set using only first-order interactions. They map the scheme to central-spin systems such as quantum dots and numerically show high logical fidelity and feasible full QEC cycles under realistic microscopic noise parameters. For large collective spins, the model predicts substantial coherence-time extension.
platform_or_system: central-spin quantum dots with collective spins
main_result: The work demonstrates a fault-tolerant collective-spin encoding with universal logical control and realistic error-correction performance using only first-order interactions.
application_angle: This could lower overhead for spin-based quantum processors by embedding error correction directly into large-spin hardware with simpler control requirements.

title: Enhancing qubit readout fidelity with two-mode squeezing of the coherent measurement signal
abstract: The ability to perform high-fidelity quantum nondemolition qubit readout is pivotal for the realization of large and powerful quantum computers. Such readout of superconducting qubits is generally enabled by amplifying the weak dispersive measurement signals using phase-preserving quantum-limited Josephson amplifiers with sufficient gain to dilute the contribution of the added noise by the output chain. Here, we further enhance the qubit readout fidelity by (1) simultaneously measuring the two-mode squeezed states of the amplified readout signals at the signal and idler frequencies of the nondegenerate amplifier and (2) coherently combining them at the classical processing stage following a relative rotation that maximizes the signal to noise ratio of the qubit-encoded readout quadrature. Such readout scheme exhibits enhancement in the readout fidelity for all practical values of amplifier gain and noise added by the output chain and is fully compatible with frequency multiplexed setups used in large quantum processors.
one_liner: Two-mode squeezing and joint signal-idler processing improve dispersive superconducting qubit readout fidelity across practical amplifier regimes.
why_it_matters: Higher-fidelity readout directly improves error correction, feedback, and scaling in multiplexed superconducting quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes enhancing superconducting qubit readout by measuring the two-mode squeezed output of a nondegenerate phase-preserving Josephson amplifier at both signal and idler frequencies. After amplification, the two channels are coherently combined in classical post-processing with an optimized relative rotation to maximize the qubit-encoded quadrature signal-to-noise ratio. The analysis shows fidelity improvement for all practical amplifier gains and output-chain added-noise levels. The method is designed to remain compatible with frequency-multiplexed readout architectures used in larger processors.
main_result: Joint processing of signal and idler modes from a nondegenerate amplifier yields consistently higher qubit readout fidelity than conventional single-channel processing.
application_angle: The scheme can be integrated into existing multiplexed superconducting readout hardware to boost measurement fidelity without changing the qubit device itself.

title: Probing keV mass QCD axions with the SACLA X-ray free electron laser
abstract: Axions are hypothetical particles, proposed to account for the invariance of CP symmetry in quantum chromodynamics. While axions and axion-like-particles are well-motivated by string theory and beyond-Standard-Model extensions, they have remained elusive to experimental searches even after significant effort over many decades. Building on a recent development using an X-ray free electron laser to search for cosmologically favoured axions of mass $m_{a} \lesssim 0.01$ eV, we extend previous bounds on the ALP-photon coupling, $g_{aγγ}$, by over an order of magnitude. We exploit the Bormann effect of Laue crystals in a light-shining-through-wall experiment, with broad sensitivity to $m_a \lesssim$ 22 eV. Moreover for $m_{a} \in$ (3460, 3480) eV our sensitivity reaches down to the QCD axion coupling prediction, providing the most stringent laboratory constraints in this mass range.
one_liner: An SACLA XFEL light-shining-through-wall search pushes ALP limits and reaches QCD-axion coupling sensitivity near 3.47 keV.
why_it_matters: It extends lab sensitivity to axion-photon coupling by over an order of magnitude and probes a narrow keV-mass window at the QCD axion target.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses the SACLA X-ray free electron laser with Laue crystals exploiting the Bormann effect to enhance photon-axion-photon conversion in a light-shining-through-wall configuration. It sets improved bounds on the axion-like-particle photon coupling for masses up to about 22 eV and reports broad sensitivity across that low-mass regime. In the narrow mass interval 3460-3480 eV, the projected or achieved sensitivity reaches the QCD axion coupling prediction, yielding the strongest laboratory constraint reported there.
main_result: The work improves ALP-photon coupling constraints by more than an order of magnitude and reaches QCD-axion-level sensitivity around 3.47 keV.
application_angle: It demonstrates that advanced X-ray photonic infrastructure can function as a precision platform for dark-matter and BSM particle searches.

title: Programmable pixel-mode linear interferometers using multi-plane light conversion
abstract: Programmable linear optical interferometers are a core primitive in optical signal processing, quantum information processing, and photonic computing. Existing photonic-integrated implementations realize arbitrary $M$-mode unitaries using Mach--Zehnder-interferometer meshes whose footprint and accumulated loss scale with $O(M^2)$ optical components. Here we analyze and experimentally demonstrate a programmable architecture for implementing linear optical transformations directly on spatially tiled free-space pixel modes using multi-plane light conversion (MPLC). In this architecture, $M$ spatial modes arranged on a transverse lattice undergo a unitary transformation and are mapped to $M$ output modes of identical geometry through a sequence of programmable phase masks separated by free-space propagation segments. Numerical simulations show that arbitrary $M$-mode unitaries can be compiled to a desired high fidelity using a number of phase planes that scales approximately linearly with $M$. Using a spatial-light-modulator-based MPLC, we experimentally demonstrate programmable interferometers acting on up to $16$ spatial pixel modes, including tunable beamsplitters, Hadamard unitaries, spatial permutations, boosted-Bell-measurement unitaries, and partial unitaries on select subsets of modes. These results establish MPLC-based pixel-mode interferometers as a promising architecture for programmable linear optics with applications in classical and quantum optical interconnects, photonic switching, and quantum information processing.
one_liner: A programmable MPLC interferometer performs high-fidelity linear optics on up to 16 spatial pixel modes with near-linear plane scaling.
why_it_matters: It offers a lower-footprint route to scalable programmable linear optics for quantum interconnects, switching, and photonic information processing.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes multi-plane light conversion as an architecture for implementing arbitrary M-mode linear optical transformations directly on spatially tiled pixel modes. Simulations indicate that the number of programmable phase planes needed for high-fidelity unitary synthesis scales approximately linearly with mode count, improving on the O(M^2) component scaling typical of MZI meshes. Using an SLM-based setup, the authors demonstrate tunable beamsplitters, Hadamards, permutations, boosted Bell-measurement unitaries, and partial unitaries on up to 16 spatial modes.
main_result: The system experimentally realizes programmable interferometers on up to 16 spatial modes and supports a range of useful quantum and classical linear-optical transformations.
application_angle: This architecture is directly relevant to reconfigurable quantum communication links, photonic switching fabrics, and scalable linear-optical processors.

title: Extreme light confinement mediated by the transverse Kerker effect
abstract: Dielectric nanoparticles can be engineered to scatter light predominantly in the transverse direction, a phenomenon known as the transverse Kerker effect. Although complete cancelation of forward scattering from a single object is forbidden by the optical theorem, we show that a single photonic mode can nonetheless realize an ideal transverse Kerker effect. The mode remains dark under normal incidence but evolves into an accidental bound state in the continuum when the nanoparticles are arranged in metasurfaces. This enables a new route to polarization-independent quasi-bound states in the continuum whose quality factors are tunable without symmetry breaking. We experimentally demonstrate our concept in the visible, achieving the first polarization-independent bound state in the continuum without the need for Brillouin-zone folding. Furthermore, we show that our modes maintain large quality factors over a substantially broader region of momentum space than conventional bound states in the continuum. Our results establish a platform for realizing ultranarrow resonances free of the constraints for designs with standard bound states in the continuum.
one_liner: A transverse-Kerker metasurface realizes polarization-independent quasi-BICs with ultranarrow visible resonances and broad momentum-space robustness.
why_it_matters: It opens a practical path to high-Q resonances without symmetry breaking, easing nanophotonic design constraints for filtering, lasing, and nonlinear optics.
detailed_summary: The work shows that a single photonic mode can exhibit an ideal transverse Kerker effect, remaining dark at normal incidence and evolving into an accidental bound state in the continuum in a nanoparticle metasurface. This enables polarization-independent quasi-BICs with tunable quality factors without relying on symmetry breaking or Brillouin-zone folding. The authors experimentally demonstrate the concept in the visible and show that the resulting modes retain large Q over a substantially wider momentum-space region than conventional BIC designs.
main_result: The paper demonstrates the first polarization-independent BIC without Brillouin-zone folding and achieves broad-angle high-Q confinement via the transverse Kerker effect.
application_angle: The resonance engineering strategy is useful for compact optical filters, sensors, low-threshold emitters, and enhanced light-matter interaction platforms.

title: Riemannian gradient descent for Hartree-Fock theory
abstract: We present a Riemannian optimization framework for Hartree-Fock theory formulated directly in the Sobolev space $H^1$. The orthonormality constraints are interpreted geometrically via infinite-dimensional Stiefel and Grassmann manifolds endowed with the embedded $H^1$ metric. Explicit expressions for Euclidean and Riemannian gradients, tangent-space projections, and retractions are derived using resolvent operators, avoiding distributional formulations. The resulting algorithms include Riemannian steepest descent and a preconditioned nonlinear conjugate gradient method equipped with Armijo backtracking and Powell-type restarts. Particular attention is given to physically motivated preconditioning based on inversion of the kinetic energy operator. The framework is naturally compatible with adaptive multiwavelet discretizations, where Coulomb-type convolutions can be evaluated efficiently. Numerical experiments demonstrate robust convergence and competitive performance compared to conventional SCF-DIIS schemes. In addition, for small molecules the gradient descent method converges from random initial guesses. The proposed formulation provides a geometrically consistent and discretization-independent perspective on electronic structure optimization and offers a foundation for further developments in infinite-dimensional Riemannian methods for quantum chemistry.
one_liner: A Sobolev-space Riemannian optimization framework delivers robust Hartree-Fock convergence using steepest descent and preconditioned nonlinear CG.
why_it_matters: It provides a geometrically consistent and potentially more reliable route to electronic-structure optimization from difficult initial conditions.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate Hartree-Fock directly on infinite-dimensional Stiefel and Grassmann manifolds equipped with the embedded H^1 metric, deriving explicit Euclidean and Riemannian gradients, projections, and retractions. Their algorithms include Riemannian steepest descent and a preconditioned nonlinear conjugate-gradient method with Armijo backtracking and Powell restarts, using kinetic-energy inversion as a physically motivated preconditioner. The framework is compatible with adaptive multiwavelet discretizations and shows robust convergence competitive with SCF-DIIS, including convergence from random initial guesses for small molecules.
main_result: The method achieves robust Hartree-Fock optimization with competitive performance versus SCF-DIIS and improved convergence behavior from challenging initial states.
application_angle: More stable quantum-chemistry solvers can accelerate computational workflows for molecular modeling relevant to materials discovery and sensing applications.

title: Simultaneous amplitude and phase spectroscopy using two-photon interference
abstract: Quantum spectroscopy seeks to probe chemical systems using nonclassical light, which has properties that are qualitatively and quantitatively different than conventional light sources. One promising technique uses intensity-correlated twin beams of light to reduce the noise sources inherent to absorption spectroscopy. However, measurements of the phase shift imparted by the chemical sample, which provides complementary information to the absorption, continue to be a challenge. Here, we propose and demonstrate a scheme using entangled photon pairs that can simultaneously measure both the absorption and phase shift of a sample with extremely low optical intensities and with relatively fast few-minute acquisition times. This method combines the previous use of intensity correlations with a broadband quantum interferometer utilizing two-photon interference to measure the complete linear optical response of the sample. Our work shows that precise measurements of absorption can also be made phase-sensitive using suitable choices of probe beam and detection scheme. This enables a new class of quantum spectroscopy schemes which measure absorption and phase with a single probe. Our technique is relevant to the characterization of a wide array of chemical and biological samples, such as quantum dots and organic fluorophores, and may be useful for spectroscopic measurements that are otherwise constrained in intensity.
one_liner: Entangled-photon two-photon interference enables simultaneous low-light measurements of sample absorption and phase in minutes.
why_it_matters: It adds phase sensitivity to quantum absorption spectroscopy, expanding low-dose characterization for fragile chemical and biological samples.
detailed_summary: The scheme combines intensity-correlated twin-beam concepts with a broadband quantum interferometer based on two-photon interference to recover the full linear optical response of a sample. Unlike conventional low-light absorption measurements, it extracts both absorption and phase shift using a single entangled-photon probe and suitable detection settings. The authors experimentally demonstrate the approach with few-minute acquisition times and extremely low optical intensities, targeting samples where probe power is constrained.
main_result: The experiment simultaneously measures absorption and phase shift of a sample using entangled photons at very low optical power and short acquisition times.
application_angle: This is well suited to spectroscopy of photosensitive quantum dots, fluorophores, and biological specimens where conventional illumination levels are damaging or impractical.

title: 3D tomography of exchange phase in a Si/SiGe quantum dot device
abstract: The exchange interaction is a foundational building block for the operation of spin-based quantum processors. Extracting the exchange interaction coefficient $J(\mathbf{V})$, as a function of gate electrode voltages, is important for understanding disorder, faithfully simulating device performance, and operating spin qubits with high fidelity. Typical coherent measurements of exchange in spin qubit devices yield a modulated cosine of an accumulated phase, which in turn is the time integral of exchange. As such, extracting $J(\mathbf{V})$ from experimental data is difficult due to the ambiguity of inverting a cosine, the sensitivity to noise when unwrapping phase, as well as the problem of inverting the integral. As a step toward obtaining $J(\mathbf{V})$, we tackle the first two challenges to reveal the accumulated phase, $φ(\mathbf{V})$. We incorporate techniques from a wide range of fields to robustly extract and model a 3D phase volume for spin qubit devices from a sequence of 2D measurements. In particular, we present a measurement technique to obtain the wrapped phase, as done in phase-shifting digital holography, and utilize the max-flow/min-cut phase unwrapping method (PUMA) to unwrap the phase in 3D voltage space. We show this method is robust to the minimal observed drift in the device, which we confirm by increasing scan resolution. Upon building a model of the extracted phase, we optimize over the model to locate a minimal-gradient $π$ exchange pulse point in voltage space. Our measurement protocol may provide detailed information useful for understanding the origins of device variability governing device yield, enable calibrating device models to specific devices during operation for more sophisticated error attribution, and enable a systematic optimization of qubit control. We anticipate that the methods presented here may be applicable to other qubit platforms.
one_liner: 3D phase tomography in a Si/SiGe spin-qubit device reconstructs exchange-phase landscapes and identifies a low-gradient pi-pulse operating point.
why_it_matters: It turns noisy, ambiguous exchange measurements into actionable 3D calibration data for higher-fidelity spin-qubit control and device diagnosis.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a measurement and reconstruction workflow for extracting the accumulated exchange phase phi(V) over a 3D gate-voltage space in a Si/SiGe quantum dot device. It adapts phase-shifting digital holography ideas to obtain wrapped phase data and applies the PUMA max-flow/min-cut algorithm to unwrap the phase volume robustly in the presence of device drift. After fitting a model to the reconstructed phase landscape, the method is used to locate a minimal-gradient pi exchange pulse point for improved qubit operation.
main_result: The authors reconstruct a robust 3D exchange-phase volume from 2D scans and use it to identify an optimized exchange-pulse operating point.
application_angle: The technique supports scalable qubit calibration, variability analysis, and model-informed control in semiconductor quantum processors.

title: Monolithic Segmented 3D Ion Trap for Quantum Technology Applications
abstract: Monolithic three-dimensional (3D) Paul traps combine the high-precision microfabrication of two-dimensional (2D) chip traps with the deep trapping potentials and low heating rates characteristic of macroscopic Paul traps, which are typically manually assembled. However, achieving low motional heating rates and optical access with a high numerical aperture (NA) while maintaining the high radio-frequency (RF) voltages required for heavy ionic species, such as Yb$^{+}$ and Ba$^{+}$, remains a significant technical challenge. In this work, we present a segmented, monolithic 3D fused silica blade trap, featuring an ion-electrode distance of 250 $μ$m with stable operation at high RF voltages. We benchmark the performance of the trap using Yb$^{+}$ ions, demonstrating axially homogeneous trapping potentials for 200 $μ$m around the axial center of the trap, high multi-directional optical access (up to 0.7 NA), and radial motional heating rate as low as 1.1 $\pm$ 0.1 quanta/s at radial trap frequencies about 3 MHz near room temperature. Furthermore, we observe a motional Ramsey coherence time, $T_{2}$, of around 95 ms for the radial center-of-mass mode. We demonstrate a two-qubit gate fidelity of ${99.3}^{+ 0.7}_{- 1.5}$$\%$ with state preparation and measurement correction. These results establish fused-silica monolithic blade traps as a scalable, modular platform for quantum simulation, computation, metrology, and networking with heavy ionic species.
one_liner: A monolithic fused-silica 3D segmented ion trap delivers low heating, high optical access, and high-fidelity two-qubit gates with Yb+ ions.
why_it_matters: It shows a manufacturable trapped-ion hardware platform that combines deep trapping, low noise, and strong optical access for computation, metrology, and networking.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a segmented monolithic 3D Paul trap made from fused silica with a 250 um ion-electrode distance and stable operation at high RF voltages suited to heavy ions such as Yb+ and Ba+. Using Yb+ ions, the authors measure axially homogeneous potentials over about 200 um, optical access up to 0.7 numerical aperture, and radial heating as low as 1.1 +/- 0.1 quanta/s near 3 MHz at room temperature. They also report a radial motional Ramsey coherence time around 95 ms and a SPAM-corrected two-qubit gate fidelity of 99.3% with asymmetric uncertainty bounds. The combination of low heating, long motional coherence, and integrated segmentation argues for scalable modular trapped-ion systems.
main_result: The system achieves low room-temperature motional heating and a 99.3% two-qubit gate fidelity in a monolithic segmented 3D ion-trap architecture.
application_angle: This architecture is directly relevant for scalable trapped-ion processors and modular quantum nodes requiring heavy-ion compatibility and large optical collection angles.

title: N-Cavity-Magnon Polariton Blockade via Kerr Nonlinearity
abstract: We theoretically propose a scheme to realize a $n$-cavity-magnon polariton blockade in a cavity-magnon system by utilizing the Kerr nonlinearity. Cavity-magnon polaritons are hybrid quasiparticles formed by the strong coupling between cavity photons and magnons. The Kerr nonlinearity introduces anharmonicity into the polariton energy spectrum, which in turn enables the blockade effect. We demonstrate that when the external driving frequency is resonant with the transition to the $n$th polariton excited state, a perfect $n$-polariton blockade is achieved. Moreover, increasing the driving strength enhances higher-order blockade while maintaining high purity. Our work pioneers the field of cavity-magnon polariton blockade, opens a new avenue for the preparation of controllable quantum resources and holds significant potential for applications in the fields of quantum communication and quantum information processing.
one_liner: A theory proposal shows Kerr nonlinearity can realize tunable n-polariton blockade in cavity-magnon systems under resonant driving.
why_it_matters: Higher-order polariton blockade could enable nonclassical state generation and engineered quantum resources for microwave quantum links and information processing.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes a strongly coupled cavity-magnon platform where Kerr nonlinearity makes the polariton ladder anharmonic, enabling blockade physics beyond the single-excitation regime. The authors show that resonantly driving the transition to the nth excited polariton state yields perfect n-polariton blockade in the model. They further find that stronger driving can enhance higher-order blockade while preserving high state purity. The proposal extends blockade engineering to cavity-magnon polaritons as a new hybrid resource platform.
main_result: The main result is a theoretical scheme for perfect and tunable n-cavity-magnon polariton blockade by matching the drive to the nth polariton transition.
application_angle: Controlled blockade in a magnon-photon hybrid platform could support on-demand nonclassical microwave resources for quantum communication and information interfaces.

title: A Scalable Open-Source QEC System with Sub-Microsecond Decoding-Feedback Latency
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for realizing large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computation, yet its practical implementation remains a major engineering challenge. In particular, QEC demands precise real-time control of a large number of qubits and low-latency, high-throughput and accurate decoding of error syndromes. While most prior work has focused primarily on decoder design, the overall performance of any QEC system depends critically on all its subsystems including control, communication, and decoding, as well as their integration. To address this challenge, we present an open-source, fully integrated QEC system built on RISC-Q, a generator for RISC-V-based quantum control architectures. Implemented on RFSoC FPGAs, our system prototype integrates real-time qubit control, a scalable distributed multi-board architecture, and the state-of-the-art hardware QEC decoder within a low-latency, high-throughput decoding pipeline, forming a complete hardware platform ready for deployment with superconducting qubits. Experimental evaluation on a three-board prototype based on AMD ZCU216 RFSoCs demonstrates an end-to-end QEC decoding-feedback latency of 446 ns for a distance-3 surface code, including syndrome aggregation, network communication, syndrome decoding, and error distribution. Extrapolating from measured subsystem performance and state-of-the-art decoder benchmarks, the architecture can achieve sub-microsecond decoding-feedback latency up to a distance-21 surface code ($\sim$881 physical qubits) when scaled to larger hardware configurations.
one_liner: An open-source RFSoC-based QEC stack demonstrates 446 ns end-to-end decoding-feedback latency for a distance-3 surface code.
why_it_matters: It addresses the real systems bottleneck in fault tolerance by integrating control, communication, and decoding into a scalable low-latency hardware path.
detailed_summary: This paper presents a full-stack quantum error correction platform built on RISC-Q, combining real-time qubit control, multi-board communication, and hardware decoding on AMD ZCU216 RFSoC boards. In a three-board prototype, the authors measure 446 ns end-to-end latency for distance-3 surface-code decoding feedback, including syndrome aggregation, network transfer, decoding, and error distribution. Based on measured subsystem scaling and decoder benchmarks, they project sub-microsecond feedback for a distance-21 surface code involving about 881 physical qubits. The contribution is notable because it treats QEC as an integrated hardware-systems problem rather than a decoder-only problem.
main_result: The prototype achieves 446 ns end-to-end decoding-feedback latency and projects sub-microsecond operation up to distance-21 surface codes.
application_angle: A practical low-latency QEC backbone is essential for deploying fault-tolerant superconducting quantum processors at useful code distances.

title: Two-Dimensional Far-Field Correlations of X-ray Photon Pairs
abstract: We directly observe far-field correlations of x-ray photon pairs generated by spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC). Using an energy-resolved, two-dimensional photon counting detector we record the full ring-shaped emission of both photons across a broad bandwidth and extract pair correlations directly from raw events without imposing angular constraints. The ring radii scale with photon energy, in quantitative agreement with transverse phase matching, providing a stringent momentum-space validation of x-ray SPDC. These observations open a route to leveraging quantum correlations in x-ray imaging and metrology, including correlation-enhanced magnification and reduced blurring.
one_liner: The authors directly map 2D far-field correlations of x-ray SPDC photon pairs and verify momentum-space phase matching across a broad bandwidth.
why_it_matters: It provides a concrete experimental basis for quantum x-ray imaging and metrology using directly observed pair correlations rather than inferred signatures.
detailed_summary: Using an energy-resolved two-dimensional photon-counting detector, the experiment records the full ring-shaped emission pattern of both photons generated by x-ray spontaneous parametric down-conversion. Pair correlations are extracted directly from raw events without imposing angular cuts, allowing a full 2D momentum-space characterization. The measured ring radii scale with photon energy in quantitative agreement with transverse phase matching, providing a stringent validation of x-ray SPDC pair emission. This is a technically important step toward leveraging quantum correlations at x-ray wavelengths.
platform_or_system: x-ray SPDC photon-pair source with 2D energy-resolved detector
main_result: The work directly observes and quantitatively validates two-dimensional far-field correlations of x-ray photon pairs over a broad spectral range.
application_angle: Verified x-ray pair correlations could enable correlation-enhanced x-ray imaging, magnification, and metrology with reduced blur and new contrast mechanisms.

title: Tunable Rotation-Associated Slow-to-Fast Light Conversion via Optomagnonic Coupling
abstract: Cavity optomechanics has enabled slow-to-fast light conversion, but traditional optomechanic systems suffer from limited tunability due to fixed mechanical frequencies. To address this constraint, we introduce a magnon degree of freedom into an optomechanical system, constructing a system that integrates photons, phonons, and magnons. We establish the theoretical model of the optomagnonic-Laguerre-Gaussian rotational system, and present numerical simulations of Fano resonances and group delay. By manipulating the magnon degree of freedom, we not only achieve slow-to-fast light conversion associated with magnons but also successfully realize such conversion effects associated with mechanical rotation-this achievement effectively overcomes the inherent tunability limitations of pure optomechanical systems and expands the frequency coverage of light conversion effects. Notably, we numerically demonstrate bidirectional light speed conversion (slow-to-fast and fast-to-slow) via continuous control field frequency modulation to tune cavity mode detuning. Additionally, our results show that adjusting optomagnonic parameters enables dynamic switching between slow light and fast light at multiple frequencies. This work provides a flexible platform for multi-frequency light speed control, with potential applications in all-optical networks and quantum communications.
one_liner: A hybrid optomagnonic-rotational system is proposed for tunable slow-to-fast and fast-to-slow light conversion across multiple frequencies.
why_it_matters: Adding magnons and rotation extends tunability beyond standard optomechanics, which is valuable for reconfigurable photonic delay and routing functions.
detailed_summary: The paper models a cavity system coupling photons, phonons, and magnons in an optomagnonic setup with Laguerre-Gaussian-driven rotational dynamics. Numerical simulations show Fano resonances and controllable group delay, with magnon tuning enabling slow-to-fast conversion not only for magnon-associated channels but also for mechanically rotational ones. By modulating the control-field frequency and optomagnonic parameters, the system can switch bidirectionally between slow and fast light at multiple frequencies. The scheme mainly contributes a broader and more flexible control space than conventional fixed-frequency optomechanics.
main_result: The main result is a tunable multi-frequency platform for reversible slow-light and fast-light conversion enabled by magnon-assisted control.
application_angle: Such reconfigurable group-delay control is relevant to all-optical networking and quantum communication links that need adaptive buffering, synchronization, or switching.

title: Regular and irregular revivals of quasi-periodic random waves
abstract: Paraxial wave packets with discrete spatial, temporal, or spatiotemporal spectra are known to undergo periodic axial revivals on propagation in either free space or linear transparent, weakly dispersive media. Such spectacular revivals, ubiquitously encountered in physics, from optics and acoustics to condensed matter physics, are distinguished by their strict periodicity. We show theoretically and verify experimentally that ensembles of quasi-periodic random wave packets exhibit a unique revival network composed of regular (periodic) and irregular (aperiodic) revivals. Moreover, individual realizations of a statistical ensemble self-reconstruct, in general, at different propagation distances than do ensemble averages. Our results shed new light on the fundamental physics of self-reconstruction of random wave packets with structured correlations.
one_liner: Theory and experiment reveal that quasi-periodic random waves exhibit both periodic and aperiodic revival networks during propagation.
why_it_matters: The result expands wave self-reconstruction physics in disordered structured fields, which can matter for robust optical transport and information encoding.
detailed_summary: The authors study paraxial wave packets with discrete but quasi-periodic spectra and show that random-wave ensembles do not simply inherit standard strictly periodic Talbot-like revival behavior. Instead, they form a revival network containing both regular periodic revivals and irregular aperiodic ones. Experiments confirm that individual realizations can self-reconstruct at propagation distances different from those of the ensemble average. This distinguishes sample-specific and ensemble-level reconstruction in structured random fields.
platform_or_system: quasi-periodic paraxial random optical wave packets
main_result: The work demonstrates experimentally that quasi-periodic random waves support mixed regular and irregular axial revivals, with realization-dependent self-reconstruction distances.
application_angle: Understanding revival structure in random optical fields can inform propagation control, resilient imaging, and correlation-based optical information transport in complex media.

title: Chipmunq: A Fault-Tolerant Compiler for Chiplet Quantum Architectures
abstract: As quantum computing advances toward fault-tolerance through quantum error correction, modular chiplet architectures have emerged to provide the massive qubit counts required while overcoming fabrication limits of monolithic chips. However, this transition introduces a critical compilation gap: existing frameworks cannot handle the scale of fault-tolerant quantum circuits while managing the noisy, sparse interconnects of chiplet backends. We present Chipmunq, the first hardware-aware compiler for mapping and routing fault-tolerant circuits onto modular architectures. Chipmunq employs a quantum-error-correction-aware partitioning strategy that preserves the integrity of logical qubit patches, preventing prohibitive gate overheads common in general-purpose compilers. Our evaluation demonstrates that Chipmunq achieves a 13.5x speedup in compilation time compared to state-of-the-art tools. By incorporating chiplet constraints and defective qubits, it reduces circuit depth by 86.4% and SWAP gate counts by 91.4% across varying code distances. Crucially, Chipmunq overcomes heterogeneous inter-chiplet links, improving logical error rates by up to two orders of magnitude.
one_liner: Chipmunq compiles fault-tolerant circuits onto modular chiplet quantum processors while accounting for sparse, noisy interconnects and defects.
why_it_matters: It closes a key toolchain gap for scalable modular quantum hardware by cutting compilation cost, routed depth, and logical failure risk.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a hardware-aware compiler for fault-tolerant circuits targeting modular chiplet-based quantum architectures. Its partitioning is quantum-error-correction aware, preserving logical qubit patch structure instead of breaking patches in ways that inflate lattice-surgery or routing overhead. Across varying code distances and heterogeneous inter-chiplet links, Chipmunq reports 13.5x faster compilation, 86.4% lower circuit depth, and 91.4% fewer SWAPs than prior tools. By explicitly modeling chiplet constraints and defective qubits, it also improves logical error rates by up to two orders of magnitude.
main_result: Chipmunq maps and routes fault-tolerant workloads on modular chiplet backends with major gains in compile time, depth, SWAP count, and logical error rate.
application_angle: The compiler makes large modular quantum processors more practical by turning real chiplet connectivity and defect constraints into deployable fault-tolerant schedules.

title: Bridging Classical Sensitivity and Quantum Scrambling: A Tutorial on Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlators
abstract: In classical dynamical systems, chaotic behavior is often associated with exponential sensitivity to initial conditions together with global phase-space structure. Translating this geometric concept to the strictly linear framework of quantum mechanics presents a conceptual puzzle. The out-of-time-ordered correlator (OTOC) is often motivated as the quantum analogue of the classical butterfly effect, but this slogan can hide important mathematical distinctions. This tutorial bridges the gap between applied mathematics and quantum information by detailing the mathematical machinery of the OTOC. We explore how classical sensitivity translates to operator non-commutativity, why standard two-point correlation functions fail to cleanly detect this sensitivity, and how the delocalization of quantum observables relates to classical notions of mixing. Crucially, we outline what the OTOC can and cannot diagnose, distinguishing between local instability and global chaos. Ultimately, we provide a precise and usable conceptual map, exploring how the Koopman-von Neumann formalism offers a framework to view classical and quantum dynamics through a shared linear perspective.
one_liner: This tutorial clarifies what out-of-time-ordered correlators do and do not reveal about quantum scrambling, chaos, and sensitivity.
why_it_matters: It gives practitioners a more precise framework for using OTOCs in experiments and simulations without overstating them as universal chaos diagnostics.
detailed_summary: The article develops the mathematical interpretation of out-of-time-ordered correlators as probes of operator non-commutativity and sensitivity to perturbations. It contrasts OTOCs with ordinary two-point correlators, explaining why the latter generally fail to capture the same notion of sensitivity, and separates local instability from global chaotic behavior. The tutorial also uses the Koopman-von Neumann formalism to place classical and quantum dynamics in a shared linear framework. The result is a technically careful map for interpreting scrambling metrics in applied quantum systems.
main_result: The work delineates the precise diagnostic scope of OTOCs, especially their relation to local sensitivity, operator delocalization, and the limits of chaos inference.
application_angle: It helps experimentalists and theorists choose and interpret scrambling observables more reliably in quantum simulation and metrology-adjacent studies.

title: Reconfigurable and Recyclable Low-Threshold Quasi-BIC Lasers via a Tunable polymer Coating
abstract: Reconfigurable and sustainable microcavity lasers are highly desirable for next-generation integrated photonics. Here, we report a recyclable, low-threshold quasi-bound state in the continuum (q-BIC) laser fabricated via low-cost, high-throughput interference lithography. By introducing a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) coating on a dye-doped photonic crystal, we suppress out-of-plane symmetry breaking, which reinforces optical confinement and reduces the lasing threshold. The q-BIC modes are further tuned through tailoring the refractive-index of the PVA layer by using Kramers-Kronig relation via Rhodamine 6G doping, demonstrating a wavelength shift of 7.14 nm and a sensitivity of 215 nm RIU as a sensing prob. More importantly, lasing modes are reversibly tuning via precisely controlling the coating thickness. Exploiting the dissolving and re-coating process, the laser is repeatedly reconfigured while maintaining performance. This work provides a sustainable and adaptive platform for sensing and reconfigurable photonic systems.
one_liner: A polymer-coated photonic crystal realizes a recyclable low-threshold quasi-BIC laser with reversible wavelength and mode tuning.
why_it_matters: It combines low-threshold lasing, reconfigurability, and reuse in one photonic device, which is attractive for adaptable integrated photonics and sensing.
detailed_summary: The device uses a dye-doped photonic crystal with a polyvinyl alcohol coating to suppress out-of-plane symmetry breaking and strengthen quasi-BIC optical confinement. This reduces the lasing threshold while enabling spectral tuning through refractive-index control of the coating, including Rhodamine 6G doping governed via the Kramers-Kronig relation. The authors report a 7.14 nm wavelength shift, 215 nm/RIU sensitivity, and reversible mode tuning through controlled coating thickness changes. Because the coating can be dissolved and reapplied, the laser can be repeatedly reconfigured without major performance loss.
platform_or_system: polymer-coated dye-doped photonic crystal q-BIC laser
main_result: The work demonstrates a recyclable quasi-BIC microcavity laser with low threshold, reversible tuning, and measurable refractive-index sensitivity.
application_angle: The approach supports reusable tunable light sources and compact on-chip sensors where post-fabrication reconfiguration and sustainability matter.

title: Optical Chopping Enhanced Rydberg-Atom-Based Ultra-Low-Frequency Electric Field Measurement
abstract: This study demonstrates a significant enhancement in ultra-low-frequency (ULF) electric field sensitivity using Rydberg atoms via an optical chopping amplification (OCA) technique. Conventional Rydberg-based ULF measurements are fundamentally limited by 1/f noise, which severely degrades sensitivity. Our approach modulates the coupling laser with an optical chopper before the vapor cell, inducing periodic Rydberg excitation at the chopping frequency. The photodetector (PD) output signal is demodulated by a lock-in amplifier (LIA) using the optical chopper's signal as the reference. This process effectively improves the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) by shifting the 1/f noise to a higher frequency band where it can be filtered out. The OCA technique enhanced sensitivity by 19.1 dB for the frequency 7 Hz, which is down to 49.1 uV/cm/rt(Hz). For the frequency range from 10Hz to 1kHz, it also enhanced nearly 7dB. This OCA method for enhancing the sensitivity of Rydberg atoms in ULF electric field measurements enables the Rydberg sensor's detection range to span the entire spectrum from low frequency (LF) to ULF, thereby significantly broadening its application potential.
one_liner: Optical chopping boosts Rydberg-atom electric-field readout by shifting ultra-low-frequency measurements away from dominant 1/f noise.
why_it_matters: It extends atom-based electric-field sensing deeper into the ultra-low-frequency regime with materially better sensitivity and broader usable bandwidth.
detailed_summary: The method modulates the coupling laser before the vapor cell so that Rydberg excitation occurs at the optical chopping frequency rather than directly at the target ultra-low-frequency field. A lock-in amplifier then demodulates the photodetector signal using the chopper reference, moving the measurement away from the strong 1/f noise floor. The reported sensitivity improvement is 19.1 dB at 7 Hz, reaching 49.1 uV/cm/sqrt(Hz), with nearly 7 dB improvement from 10 Hz to 1 kHz. This effectively broadens the operating range of Rydberg electric-field sensors from low frequency into the ULF band.
main_result: Optical chopping amplification improves ULF Rydberg electric-field sensitivity to 49.1 uV/cm/sqrt(Hz) at 7 Hz and yields gains across 10 Hz to 1 kHz.
application_angle: The technique makes atom-based E-field probes more viable for low-frequency electromagnetic monitoring where conventional readout is limited by technical noise.

title: Achieving Sub-Zeptonewton Force Sensitivity and Spin-Motion Entanglement in Levitated Diamond via Pulsed Backaction Evasion
abstract: We propose a system to achieve sub-zeptonewton force sensing and robust spin-mechanical entanglement in a levitated diamond system. By coupling a Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) center spin to the motion of its host diamond within a magnetic trap, we develop a platform designed to surpass the standard quantum limit. We develop and compare three distinct pulse sequences--Ramsey, Hahn echo, and Carr-Purcell-Meiboom-Gill (CPMG)--to create increasing amounts of backaction evasion while mitigating the effects of shot noise and thermal decoherence. Our results show that the CPMG sequences yield the most significant performance gains, reaching a force sensitivity of better than $10^{-23} \text{ N}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ for broadband sensing around $10^4 \text{ Hz}$. Furthermore, we derive an entanglement witness protocol that accounts for pulsed dynamical decoupling, proving that spin-motion entanglement remains detectable even when occurring much faster than the mechanical period. These findings provide a more practical path for using levitated nanodiamonds both as high-precision sensors and as non-classical mechanical systems for fundamental tests of quantum mechanics.
one_liner: Pulsed backaction-evasion protocols in levitated diamond promise sub-zeptonewton force sensing and detectable spin-motion entanglement.
why_it_matters: It outlines a realistic route to quantum-limited sensing and nonclassical control in a mechanically isolated solid-state platform.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes an NV-center spin coupled to the center-of-mass motion of a levitated diamond in a magnetic trap, focusing on pulsed protocols that evade measurement backaction. Ramsey, Hahn-echo, and CPMG sequences are compared, with CPMG giving the strongest suppression of shot-noise and thermal-decoherence effects. The projected force sensitivity is better than 10^-23 N/sqrt(Hz) around 10^4 Hz, and the authors derive an entanglement-witness method compatible with dynamical decoupling even when entanglement develops faster than a mechanical period. The proposal therefore connects ultrasensitive force metrology with experimentally accessible spin-mechanical nonclassicality.
main_result: CPMG-based pulsed backaction evasion enables predicted sub-zeptonewton force sensitivity and preserves observable spin-motion entanglement in levitated diamond.
application_angle: This could enable compact force sensors and precision probes while also supporting macroscopic quantum tests in a solid-state levitated platform.

title: Distinguishing types of correlated errors in superconducting qubits
abstract: Errors in superconducting qubits that are correlated in time and space can pose problems for quantum error correction codes. Radiation from cosmic and terrestrial sources can increase the quasiparticle (QP) density in a superconducting qubit device, resulting in an increased rate of QPs tunneling across proximal Josephson junctions (JJs) and causing correlated errors. Mechanical vibrations, such as those induced by the pulse tube in a dry dilution refrigerator, are also a known source of correlated errors. We present a method for distinguishing these two types of errors by their temporal, spatial, and frequency domain features, enabling physically motivated error-mitigation strategies. We also present accelerometer data to study the correlation between dilution refrigerator vibrations and the errors. We measure arrays of transmon qubits where the difference in superconducting gap across the JJ is less than the qubit energy, as well as those where the gap is greater than the qubit energy, which has been shown to mitigate radiation-induced errors. We show that these latter devices are also protected against vibration-induced errors.
one_liner: The paper separates radiation-driven and vibration-driven correlated errors in transmons using temporal, spatial, and spectral signatures.
why_it_matters: Identifying error origin is essential for targeted mitigation in superconducting processors where correlated faults can undermine error correction.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a method to distinguish two important correlated-error channels in superconducting qubits: quasiparticle bursts induced by radiation and mechanically driven errors linked to cryostat vibrations. They analyze temporal, spatial, and frequency-domain features of measured error events and compare them with accelerometer data from the dilution refrigerator. The study includes transmon arrays with different superconducting-gap engineering across Josephson junctions, leveraging designs known to suppress radiation-induced quasiparticle tunneling. Devices with gap differences exceeding the qubit energy are shown to resist not only radiation-induced events but also vibration-induced errors.
main_result: The work provides an experimental framework to classify correlated transmon errors by physical origin and shows gap-engineered devices suppress both radiation and vibration effects.
application_angle: This supports hardware-specific mitigation strategies and more reliable fault-tolerance planning for superconducting quantum processors.

title: High Fidelity Single-NV Qubit Quantum State Tomography by Photoelectric Readout
abstract: Quantum computing is a rapidly developing field. However, the most commonly used qubits require cryogenic conditions to operate, which increases the costs and puts constraints on the up-scaling. Ambient solid-state qubits provide an alternative with potential for large-scale application. The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond is one of the main candidates for solid-state computing architectures at room temperature and has proven to be competitive in terms of gate fidelity, quantum error correction, couplings, etc. Each NV center has an associated electronic spin that is conventionally read out by photoluminescence. However, regarding the creation of small, ambient NV-based quantum processors, the optical readout introduces limitations on the collection efficiency and resolution of the readout as well as the size of the final device and its integration into standard semiconductor architectures. In this work, we investigate the competitiveness of the photoelectric readout versus the traditional optical readout. In particular, we report on using photoelectrical detection to perform quantum state tomography measurements on a single NV center. We achieve the fidelity $0.995 \pm 0.0062$ for state reconstruction, comparable to optical measurements, demonstrating that the fidelity does not suffer from the adapted readout, highlighting the value of photoelectric detection for NV-based quantum processors.
one_liner: Single-NV spin state tomography via photoelectric readout reaches optical-grade fidelity, supporting compact room-temperature diamond qubit control.
why_it_matters: It shows electrically compatible NV readout can match optical tomography fidelity, removing a key integration bottleneck for scalable ambient solid-state quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The work performs quantum state tomography on a single nitrogen-vacancy center using photoelectrical rather than photoluminescence readout. It reports a reconstructed-state fidelity of 0.995 ± 0.0062, showing that switching to electrical detection does not significantly degrade single-spin measurement quality. The comparison is directly relevant for miniaturized NV processors because photoelectric readout can reduce optical collection and integration constraints. The result strengthens the case for semiconductor-compatible, room-temperature NV qubit architectures.
main_result: Photoelectric readout enabled single-NV quantum state tomography with 0.995 ± 0.0062 fidelity, comparable to conventional optical readout.
application_angle: Electrical readout is more amenable to dense on-chip integration, making compact ambient NV quantum processors and sensors more practical.

title: Stroboscopic detection of itinerant microwave photons
abstract: We present a novel scheme to detect itinerant microwave radiation at the single photon level. Using existing Josephson-photonics devices, where two microwave cavities are coupled by a dc-voltage biased superconducting junction, we theoretically show how to implement a stroboscopically repeated, near-projective measurement of a photon impinging on one of the cavities. Optimizing rate, duration, and strength of the measurement by flux control of the junction and developing a threshold protocol to detect the photon from a homodyne measurement of the radiation output of the other cavity, we achieve highly efficient detection with low dark counts. By cascading the detector with a preamplifier, where a similar two-cavity Josephson-photonics device acts as a photon multiplier, we can further improve the device to reach a detection efficiency of $88.5 \%$ with a dark count rate of $\sim10^{-4} γ_a$, set by the resonance width $γ_a$ of the absorbing cavity. These results for a multiplication factor of two suggest that near-unity efficiencies may be reached for higher multiplication factors.
one_liner: A Josephson-photonics scheme enables stroboscopic near-projective detection of single itinerant microwave photons with low dark counts.
why_it_matters: Efficient traveling microwave-photon detection is a missing component for superconducting quantum networks, and this design approaches useful performance with existing hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a detector for itinerant microwave photons based on two microwave cavities coupled by a dc-biased superconducting junction. By flux-tuning the junction, the authors implement repeated stroboscopic measurements and infer photon arrival with a threshold protocol on the second cavity's homodyne output. In a cascaded configuration with a photon-multiplying preamplifier, the modeled detector reaches 88.5% efficiency with a dark-count rate of about 10^-4 γ_a for multiplication factor two. The analysis suggests that higher multiplication factors could push efficiency close to unity.
main_result: A stroboscopic Josephson-photonics detector is predicted to achieve 88.5% single-microwave-photon detection efficiency with very low dark counts when cascaded with a multiplier.
application_angle: Reliable detection of propagating microwave photons would directly support superconducting quantum communication links, remote entanglement, and modular cryogenic processors.

title: Logarithmic-depth quantum state preparation of polynomials
abstract: Quantum state preparation is a central primitive in many quantum algorithms, yet it is generally resource intensive, with efficient constructions known only for structured families of states. This work introduces a method for preparing quantum states whose amplitudes are given by a degree$-d$ polynomial, using circuits with logarithmic depth in the number $n$ of qubits and only $\mathcal O(n)$ ancilla qubits, improving previous approaches that required linear-depth circuits. The construction first relies on a block-encoding of an affine diagonal operator based on its Pauli-basis decomposition, which involves only $n$ terms. A modified linear-combination-of-unitaries (LCU) technique is introduced to implement this decomposition in logarithmic depth, together with a novel circuit for the EXACT-one oracle that flags basis states in which exactly one qubit is in the state $|1\rangle$. It then uses a generalized quantum eigenvalue transformation (GQET) to promote this affine operator to an arbitrary degree polynomial. Theoretical analysis and numerical simulations are reported along with a proof-of-principle implementation on a trapped-ion quantum processor using $14$ qubits and more than $500$ primitive quantum gates. Because polynomial approximations are ubiquitous in scientific computing, this construction provides a scalable and resource-efficient approach to quantum state preparation, further improving the potential of quantum algorithms in fields such as chemistry, physics, engineering, and finance.
one_liner: A logarithmic-depth circuit family prepares polynomial-amplitude quantum states and is demonstrated on a 14-qubit trapped-ion processor.
why_it_matters: Reducing state-preparation depth from linear to logarithmic improves feasibility on near-term hardware for many algorithms that rely on polynomial approximations.
detailed_summary: The authors present a construction for preparing n-qubit states whose amplitudes follow a degree-d polynomial using logarithmic circuit depth and O(n) ancillas. The approach combines a Pauli-basis block encoding of an affine diagonal operator, a modified low-depth LCU method, an EXACT-one oracle, and generalized quantum eigenvalue transformation to synthesize higher-degree polynomials. Numerical analysis is paired with a proof-of-principle trapped-ion implementation using 14 qubits and more than 500 primitive gates. The work targets a central bottleneck in quantum algorithms where structured amplitude loading dominates resource costs.
main_result: The method prepares polynomial-amplitude states in logarithmic depth in qubit number, improving on prior linear-depth constructions and demonstrating feasibility on 14 trapped-ion qubits.
application_angle: More efficient amplitude loading can lower hardware overhead for quantum simulations, optimization, and scientific-computing workloads that use polynomial approximations.

title: CryoCMOS RF multiplexer for superconducting qubit control, readout and flux biasing at millikelvin temperatures with picowatt power consumption
abstract: Large-scale cryogenic quantum systems are constrained by an input-output bottleneck between room-temperature electronics and millikelvin stages, particularly in superconducting qubit platforms. This bottleneck is most acute for output lines, where bulky and expensive microwave components limit scalability. A promising approach for scalable characterization and testing is to perform signal multiplexing directly at the qubit plane. We demonstrate a cryogenic CMOS (cryoCMOS) RF multiplexer operating at 10 millikelvin with record-low static power consumption of 200 pW. The device provides < 2 dB insertion loss and > 30 dB isolation across DC-8 GHz. Direct connection to transmon qubits marginally affects coherence times in the range of 100 microseconds, enabling multiplexing of readout, flux and, in principle, XY drive lines. This work introduces cryoCMOS multiplexers as valuable tools for scalable, high-throughput cryogenic characterization and testing, and advances co-integrated quantum-classical control for future large-scale quantum processors.
one_liner: A 10 mK cryoCMOS RF multiplexer routes qubit signals with picowatt static power while preserving transmon coherence near 100 microseconds.
why_it_matters: Millikelvin multiplexing directly attacks wiring and component bottlenecks that currently limit scaling and test throughput in superconducting qubit systems.
detailed_summary: This work demonstrates a cryogenic CMOS RF multiplexer operating at 10 mK with only 200 pW static power dissipation. Across DC to 8 GHz, the device shows less than 2 dB insertion loss and more than 30 dB isolation, making it suitable for routing readout, flux, and potentially XY control lines. When directly connected to transmon qubits, coherence is only marginally affected, with T-times remaining in the roughly 100 microsecond range. The result is a concrete hardware step toward co-integrated cryogenic control infrastructure.
main_result: The authors realize a millikelvin cryoCMOS RF multiplexer with 200 pW static power, <2 dB loss, >30 dB isolation, and minimal impact on transmon coherence.
application_angle: On-chip multiplexing can cut cryostat I/O complexity and cost, enabling higher-throughput characterization and more scalable superconducting quantum processor packaging.

title: Quantum dynamics of few-photon pulsed waveguide-QED with a single artificial atom: frequency-dependent scattering theory and time-dependent matrix product states
abstract: We present a quantum dynamical study of pulsed few-photon scattering from a single artificial atom, consisting of a two-level system (TLS) or qubit, in a waveguide QED system, directly comparing and contrasting two different quantum theoretical simulation methods: (i) an input-output scattering approach that uses frequency-dependent scattering matrices, and (ii) a matrix product states (MPS) approach, which uses quantum noise operators in time bins and a tensor network technique to solve the time-dependent waveguide function for the entire system. Beginning with pulsed excitation using one-photon and two-photon Fock state pulses, we first show how to compute time-dependent observables with the scattering matrix approach, in terms of frequency integrals that encode the pulse spectrum, including how to extract the population dynamics of the excited quantum emitter, as well as the linear and nonlinear contributions. We present solutions for both symmetric and chiral TLS coupling. We then show how to compute the qubit and field observables in a more direct way using MPS, and obtain the characteristic bird-like shape for the two-photon correlation function at two times, which has been observed in recent experiments. We compare and contrast both of these methods, for one and two-photon excitation pulses, and show excellent agreement. We also present a study of the linear and nonlinear contributions, which can easily be calculated using scattering theory, and show the important role of pulse duration. Finally, we demonstrate the clear advantages of MPS by easily going to higher N-photon excitations, and show selected example population dynamics of up to eight-photon Fock-state pulses, manifesting in clear nonlinear population oscillations during the pulse interaction, similar to classical Rabi oscillations, but with quantum input fields that have a vanishing electric field expectation value.
one_liner: Waveguide-QED few-photon pulse dynamics are resolved by matching frequency-domain scattering theory to time-dependent MPS simulations.
why_it_matters: Validated tools for pulsed few-photon scattering are essential for designing quantum photonic interfaces, nonlinear routers, and microwave-optical network components.
detailed_summary: The paper compares two complementary methods for pulsed few-photon scattering from a single artificial atom in a waveguide: frequency-dependent input-output scattering matrices and time-bin matrix product states. For one- and two-photon Fock pulses, both methods agree on emitter population dynamics and time-resolved field observables, including the experimentally observed bird-like two-time correlation pattern. The analysis separates linear and nonlinear scattering contributions and shows the importance of pulse duration. MPS then extends naturally to higher-photon inputs, with simulations up to eight-photon pulses revealing strong nonlinear population oscillations.
platform_or_system: single artificial atom in waveguide QED
main_result: Scattering theory and MPS give consistent time-dependent predictions for pulsed few-photon waveguide-QED dynamics, while MPS further captures nonlinear behavior for up to eight-photon inputs.
application_angle: The framework helps engineer single-emitter quantum photonic devices by predicting pulse-shape-dependent nonlinear response and correlation signatures relevant to quantum networking components.

title: FAlCon: A unified framework for algorithmic control of quantum dot devices
abstract: As spin-based quantum systems scale, their setup and control complexity increase sharply. In semiconductor quantum dot (QD) experiments, device-to-device variability, heterogeneous control-electronics stacks, and differing operational modalities make it difficult to reuse characterization, calibration, and control logic across laboratories. We present FAlCon, an open-source software ecosystem for portable, automated characterization and tuning measurement workflows. FAlCon provides (i) a lightweight domain-specific language for expressing state-based tuning logic in a hardware-agnostic form; (ii) specialized transmittable libraries of physics-informed QD data structures (``tuning vernacula''); and (iii) extensible libraries of shared measurement protocols enabling an interoperable lab-agnostic measurement stack. By separating algorithm intent from instrument realization, while preserving traceability and supporting typed scripting, FAlCon enables researchers and engineers to exchange, adapt, and deploy characterization and autotuning routines across heterogeneous QD setups. The framework supports all users, ranging from end users running prebuilt algorithms with custom initial conditions to developers extending instrumentation support and contributing new tuning strategies. Although the present release targets QD experiments, other qubit modalities and scientific experiments could reuse FAlCon's modular abstractions by providing new tuning data types and instrument control templates.
one_liner: FAlCon provides a portable, hardware-agnostic software stack for automated tuning and control of semiconductor quantum dot devices.
why_it_matters: Reusable autotuning workflows are critical for scaling spin-qubit experiments beyond bespoke lab-specific control stacks and manual calibration loops.
detailed_summary: FAlCon is an open-source framework for expressing and deploying quantum-dot characterization and tuning routines across heterogeneous experimental setups. Its architecture combines a lightweight domain-specific language for state-based tuning logic, physics-informed transferable data structures, and extensible measurement protocol libraries that decouple algorithm intent from instrument implementation. The framework is designed for both end users running packaged routines and developers extending hardware support or adding new strategies. Although focused on semiconductor quantum dots, the abstractions are intended to generalize to other qubit modalities.
main_result: The authors introduce a unified software ecosystem that makes quantum-dot tuning logic portable, typed, traceable, and reusable across different labs and control electronics.
