application_angle: Standardized autotuning infrastructure can accelerate device bring-up, reduce expert intervention, and improve reproducibility for larger spin-qubit hardware programs.

title: Secure Quantum Communication: Simulation and Analysis of Quantum Key Distribution Protocols
abstract: Quantum computing poses significant threats to conventional cryptographic techniques such as RSA and AES, motivating the need for quantum secure communication methods. Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) offers information theoretic security based on fundamental quantum principles. This paper presents a simulation-based analysis of well-known QKD protocols, namely BB84, B92, and E91, using the IBM Qiskit framework. Realistic quantum channel effects, including noise, decoherence, and eavesdropping, are modeled to evaluate protocol performance. Key metrics such as error rate, secret key generation, and security characteristics are analyzed and compared. The study highlights practical challenges in QKD implementation, including hardware limitations and channel losses, and discusses insights toward scalable and robust quantum communication systems. The results support the feasibility of QKD as a promising solution for secure communication in the quantum era.
one_liner: Qiskit simulations compare BB84, B92, and E91 under noise, decoherence, and eavesdropping to benchmark practical QKD performance.
why_it_matters: It gives an implementation-focused view of how leading QKD protocols degrade in realistic channels, informing protocol choice and deployment tradeoffs.
detailed_summary: The work simulates three canonical QKD protocols, BB84, B92, and E91, within IBM Qiskit while explicitly incorporating noise, decoherence, loss, and eavesdropping. It evaluates quantum bit error rate, secret key generation, and security behavior across the protocols rather than treating them in an idealized setting. The comparison emphasizes how channel impairments and hardware constraints shape achievable secure throughput and robustness in practical links.
main_result: The paper shows, via realistic simulation, that BB84, B92, and E91 remain feasible for secure key distribution but exhibit distinct error-rate and secret-key tradeoffs under nonideal channel conditions.
application_angle: It is useful for selecting and engineering QKD protocols for deployable secure fiber or networked quantum communication systems.

title: A Compact Broadband Purcell Filter for Superconducting Quantum Circuits in a 3D Flip-Chip Architecture
abstract: Fast and high-fidelity qubit readout requires strong coupling between the readout resonator and the feedline. However, such coupling unavoidably enhances qubit decay through the Purcell effect. We present a four-pole broadband Purcell filter implemented on a 3D flip-chip platform to overcome this trade-off. The filter provides a flat 1 GHz passband centered at 7.68 GHz and achieves more than 45 dB suppression at typical qubit frequencies. We demonstrate the filter's compatibility with multiplexed readout using a test chip that integrates six floating readout resonators strongly coupled within the passband. The chip is fabricated using a 150 nm Niobium (Nb) thin-film process and characterized at 20 mK in a cryogenic measurement setup. We also develop an analytical model that accurately captures the filter response and determines the resonance frequencies and external quality factors of the floating resonators directly from their physical geometry, enabling rapid circuit synthesis and design optimization. The proposed design is compact and fabrication-tolerant, making it a practical solution for large-scale superconducting quantum processors.
one_liner: A four-pole broadband Purcell filter in 3D flip-chip superconducting hardware enables strong readout coupling while suppressing qubit decay.
why_it_matters: It addresses a central scaling bottleneck in superconducting processors by improving multiplexed readout without sacrificing qubit lifetime.
detailed_summary: The authors implement a compact four-pole Purcell filter on a 3D flip-chip platform for superconducting circuits. The device provides a flat 1 GHz passband centered at 7.68 GHz while delivering more than 45 dB suppression at typical qubit frequencies, separating fast resonator readout from qubit relaxation channels. A six-resonator test chip fabricated in 150 nm Nb is measured at 20 mK, and an analytical geometry-to-response model is developed for rapid synthesis of resonance frequencies and external quality factors.
main_result: The filter demonstrates broadband compatibility with multiplexed readout together with strong suppression of Purcell-limited decay in a compact, fabrication-tolerant design.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to scaling superconducting quantum processors where dense, high-fidelity, multi-qubit readout must coexist with long qubit lifetimes.

title: Efficient generation of entangled photons in the telecommunications range using nonlinear metasurfaces integrated with ScAlN/GaN heterostructures
abstract: Entangled photons provide non-classical correlations that enable measurement sensitivities beyond classical limits, scalable fault-tolerant quantum computation, and fundamentally secure quantum communication, making them a foundational necessity for next-generation quantum technologies. Here we propose and analyze a novel source of entangled photons based on ScAlN/GaN quantum wells integrated with dielectric metasurfaces. Giant second-order intersubband nonlinearity of the GaN quantum wells with strain-compensated delta-doped ScAlN barriers caused by strong built-in electric fields combined with superior mode-coupling performance of metasurfaces optimized by inverse design give rise to efficient parametric down-conversion and generation of entangled photons in the telecom range. We develop a rigorous Heisenberg-Langevin formalism which includes field quantization, dissipation and fluctuations for all fields, parametric amplification of thermal noise and zero-point fluctuations, and other relevant effects. Our proposed approach of employing the emergent photonic material ScAlN promises high biphoton generation rate over $10^{10}$ s$^{-1}$ from a compact integrated structure that is only 0.5 $μ$m thick while mitigating strain-related issues that have so far impeded progress of nitride-based heterostructures for quantum photonic applications into the infrared and visible wavelengths. Our result therefore is relevant for numerous applications ranging from quantum sensing, quantum information, and computing.
one_liner: ScAlN/GaN quantum wells with inverse-designed metasurfaces are proposed as ultrathin telecom-band entangled-photon sources with very high pair rates.
why_it_matters: It points to a compact integrated route to bright telecom entangled-photon generation using nitride heterostructures compatible with sensing and networking applications.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a biphoton source based on ScAlN/GaN quantum wells integrated with dielectric metasurfaces, leveraging giant second-order intersubband nonlinearity from built-in electric fields in strain-compensated heterostructures. An explicit Heisenberg-Langevin treatment includes quantization, dissipation, fluctuations, and thermal-noise amplification to model parametric down-conversion rigorously. The design predicts telecom-band entangled-photon generation rates above 10^10 s^-1 from an integrated structure only about 0.5 um thick.
main_result: The main result is a theoretically analyzed ultrathin integrated source that can generate telecom entangled photons at predicted rates exceeding 10^10 pairs per second.
application_angle: A bright, compact telecom source is attractive for chip-scale quantum communications, photonic interconnects, and nonclassical sensing platforms.

title: High-rate quantum digital signatures over 250 km of optical fiber
abstract: Quantum digital signatures (QDS) offer information-theoretic security for message integrity, authenticity, and non-repudiation, and constitute a fundamental cryptographic primitive for future quantum networks. Despite significant progress, the practical deployment of QDS has been severely constrained by limited signature rates and poor tolerance to channel loss, particularly in long-distance and metropolitan-scale networks. Here, we report a high-rate, loss-resilient QDS system that overcomes these two key bottlenecks simultaneously. Our implementation combines intrinsically phase-stable polarization modulation based on a Sagnac interferometer with gigahertz-rate quantum state encoding and low-timing-jitter superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors, enabling robust and continuous operation at high repetition frequencies. By integrating this hardware platform with a one-time universal hashing-based QDS protocol, we achieve a signature rate improvement of more than two orders of magnitude compared with existing QDS implementations under comparable channel-loss conditions. Notably, the system maintains a non-zero effective signature rate of approximately 1.25 times per second at a total channel loss of up to 49.05 dB, representing the highest loss tolerance reported for QDS to date. These results establish a practical and scalable technological pathway for deploying QDS in real-world quantum communication networks.
one_liner: A gigahertz-rate QDS system achieves high signature throughput over 250 km fiber and record loss tolerance using stable encoding and SNSPDs.
why_it_matters: It moves quantum digital signatures closer to deployable quantum-network security by overcoming the two major barriers of low rate and high loss.
detailed_summary: This experiment combines intrinsically phase-stable polarization modulation in a Sagnac interferometer, gigahertz-rate state encoding, and low-jitter superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors. Integrated with a one-time universal hashing QDS protocol, the platform improves signature rate by more than two orders of magnitude versus prior implementations at similar losses. It maintains a nonzero effective signature rate of about 1.25 s^-1 up to 49.05 dB total channel loss, corresponding to 250 km optical fiber and the highest reported QDS loss tolerance.
platform_or_system: Fiber-based QDS with Sagnac encoding and SNSPDs
main_result: The system demonstrates high-rate quantum digital signatures over 250 km of fiber with nonzero throughput at 49.05 dB loss, setting a new loss-tolerance benchmark.
application_angle: It is directly relevant for authentication, integrity, and non-repudiation services in metropolitan and long-haul quantum communication networks.

title: Boosted linear-optical measurements on single-rail qubits with unentangled ancillas
abstract: Any quantum state of the radiation field, sliced in small non-overlapping space-time bins is a collection of single-rail qubits, each spanning the vacuum and single-photon Fock state of a mode. Quantum logic on these qubits would enable arbitrary measurements on information-bearing light, but is hard due to the lack of strong nonlinearities. With unentangled ancilla single-rail qubits, an $8$-port interferometer and photon detection, we show any single-rail qubit measurement in the $XY$ Bloch plane is realizable with success probability $147/256$, which beats the prior-known $1/2$ limit.
one_liner: Linear optics with unentangled single-rail ancillas and an 8-port interferometer boosts arbitrary XY-plane single-rail measurements beyond the 1/2 limit.
why_it_matters: It expands what can be done with experimentally accessible linear-optical resources for photonic qubit processing and measurement.
detailed_summary: The authors study measurement of single-rail qubits, where each mode encodes a qubit in the vacuum and one-photon subspace. Using only unentangled ancillary single-rail qubits, an 8-port interferometer, and photon counting, they show that any qubit measurement in the XY plane of the Bloch sphere can be implemented with success probability 147/256. This exceeds the previously known 1/2 bound for such linear-optical measurement schemes.
main_result: The paper proves a success probability of 147/256 for arbitrary XY-plane measurements on single-rail qubits using only unentangled ancillas and linear optics.
application_angle: Improved measurement primitives can strengthen near-term photonic information processing and communication protocols without requiring strong optical nonlinearities.

title: The weakly interacting tenfold way
authors: Lucas C. P. A. M. Müssnich
abstract: We present implementations of the topological K-theory spectra $KU$ and $KO$ in terms of time evolution operators of irreducible free fermion systems with symmetries, with explicit formulas for the structural suspension maps. We also introduce a geometric definition of weakly interacting time evolution operators, and show how associated spectra $KU^{wi}$ and $KO^{wi}$ deformation retract to $KU$ and $KO$. We thus have a stable homotopy theoretical proof that the tenfold way is stable to weak interactions.
one_liner: A stable-homotopy treatment shows the free-fermion tenfold-way classification persists under weak interactions via deformation-retract spectra.
why_it_matters: It gives a mathematically rigorous stability result for topological phase classification, clarifying when weak interactions preserve hardware-relevant free-fermion structure.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs implementations of the K-theory spectra KU and KO using time-evolution operators of irreducible symmetric free-fermion systems, with explicit suspension maps. It then defines weakly interacting time-evolution operators geometrically and introduces corresponding spectra KU^wi and KO^wi. By proving these weakly interacting spectra deformation retract to the free-fermion spectra, the authors establish that the tenfold-way classification is stable to weak interactions.
main_result: The main result is a proof that weakly interacting fermionic systems retain the same stable topological classification as the free-fermion tenfold way.
application_angle: This informs design confidence for topological quantum materials and devices by identifying a regime where interaction effects do not invalidate the standard classification.

title: Measurement-Based Estimation of Causal Conditional Variances and Its Application to Macroscopic quantum phenomenon
abstract: We analytically investigate a quantum estimation method for a mechanical oscillator in a detuned cavity system based solely on homodyne measurement records, building on the framework developed by C.Meng et al. (Science Advances 8, 7585 (2022)). Estimation based only on measurement records is important because it enables state verification without assuming knowledge of the true system state. We construct a relative estimate operator from causal and anti-causal quantum Wiener filters and calculate its variance. The deviation from the causal conditional variance is defined as a reconstruction bias, whose magnitude is evaluated analytically. We show that, within experimentally relevant parameter regimes for typical quantum-state preparation, the reconstruction bias is sufficiently small to be neglected. As applications to state verification, we apply the method to proposals for macroscopic quantum entanglement mediated by electromagnetic interactions and for conditional momentum-squeezed states generated by homodyne detection, and clarify the conditions under which the bias remains negligible and when the reconstruction bias becomes significant.
one_liner: A homodyne-record-only estimator can verify optomechanical conditional states with analytically bounded bias in realistic detuned cavity regimes.
why_it_matters: It strengthens measurement-only verification for macroscopic quantum states by showing when reconstruction bias is negligible in experimentally relevant optomechanics.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a relative estimate operator for a mechanical oscillator using causal and anti-causal quantum Wiener filters applied only to homodyne measurement records from a detuned cavity. It analytically separates the causal conditional variance from a reconstruction bias and shows that the bias is typically negligible in parameter regimes used for quantum-state preparation. The framework is then applied to macroscopic entanglement proposals and conditional momentum squeezing, identifying when record-based verification remains trustworthy and when bias becomes appreciable.
main_result: The authors show that measurement-record-only reconstruction can accurately estimate conditional variances in realistic detuned optomechanical systems, with small bias over relevant operating regimes.
application_angle: This gives experimental groups a practical route to verify macroscopic quantum states and squeezing without relying on assumed knowledge of the underlying true state.

title: Chiral and bond-ordered phases in a triangular-ladder superconducting-qubit quantum simulator
abstract: Many-body systems with strong interactions often exhibit macroscopic behavior markedly absent in single-particle or noninteracting limits. Such emergent phenomena are well exemplified in lattice Hubbard models, where the interplay between interactions, geometric frustration, and magnetic flux gives rise to rich physics. Superconducting qubits naturally enable analog quantum simulation of Bose-Hubbard models, while offering tunable parameters, site-resolved control, and rapid experimental repetition rates. Here, we study a superconducting-qubit device that realizes the Bose-Hubbard model on a triangular-ladder lattice. By tuning the magnitude and sign of couplings, we engineer a synthetic magnetic flux to characterize the resulting half-filling ground state for various parameter regimes. We measure observables analogous to current-current correlators and bond kinetic energies, finding signatures consistent with chiral superfluids, Meissner superfluids, and bond-ordered insulators. Our results establish superconducting circuits as a platform for robustly probing quantum phases of matter in frustrated Bose-Hubbard systems, even in strongly correlated and gapless regimes.
one_liner: A triangular-ladder superconducting-qubit simulator realizes frustrated Bose-Hubbard physics with signatures of chiral, Meissner, and bond-ordered phases.
why_it_matters: It expands superconducting-circuit analog simulation into frustrated, flux-engineered many-body regimes that are hard to access classically and experimentally valuable.
detailed_summary: Using a superconducting-qubit device implementing a triangular-ladder Bose-Hubbard model, the authors tune coupling magnitudes and signs to synthesize magnetic flux. They probe the half-filled ground state with observables analogous to current-current correlators and bond kinetic energies. The measured response is consistent with chiral superfluids, Meissner superfluids, and bond-ordered insulators, including strongly correlated and gapless parameter regimes.
main_result: The device experimentally accesses multiple frustration-driven Bose-Hubbard phases by engineering synthetic flux on a triangular-ladder lattice.
application_angle: The work positions superconducting circuits as a programmable hardware platform for studying correlated lattice matter and benchmarking analog quantum simulators beyond simple geometries.

title: Quantum memory precludes mixed-unitary dynamics
abstract: Unital quantum channels, defined by their property of leaving the maximally mixed state invariant, form an important class of quantum operations. A distinguished subset of these channels can be represented as a probabilistic mixture of unitary evolutions. Characterizing channels that do not admit such a decomposition is in general a hard problem with significant implications for noise mitigation in quantum technologies and for fundamental problems in quantum information theory. Here we establish a link between mixed-unitarity of unital channels and the (quantum) nature of the memory effects in non-Markovian dynamics. Translating the problem into the language of process tensors, this connection yields a hierarchy of semidefinite programs that provides numerically efficient witnesses for non-mixed-unitary behavior, outperforming existing criteria. We demonstrate the power of this approach through illustrative examples of unital channels in dimensions three and four.
one_liner: A process-tensor hierarchy links quantum memory in non-Markovian dynamics to witnesses of non-mixed-unitary unital channels.
why_it_matters: It provides a more efficient way to certify when unital noise cannot be modeled as random unitary evolution, which is important for mitigation and channel characterization.
detailed_summary: The work reformulates mixed-unitarity testing for unital quantum channels in the language of process tensors, exposing a direct connection to genuinely quantum memory effects in non-Markovian dynamics. From this, the authors build a hierarchy of semidefinite programs that acts as a witness family for non-mixed-unitary behavior. They demonstrate stronger performance than existing criteria on representative qutrit and four-dimensional channel examples.
main_result: The paper establishes that quantum memory effects can witness non-mixed-unitary unital dynamics and turns this into a practical SDP-based certification hierarchy.
application_angle: Better certification of realistic noise models can improve quantum-network channel assessment and inform mitigation strategies that assume or exploit unitary-mixture structure.

title: Bell Inequalities for Smells
abstract: In this work, we study a particular class of Bell inequalities involving only direct equality-comparisons of outcomes. This arises naturally when outcomes are difficult to characterize. For instance, if measurements yield smells, it may be impractical to process them individually, while still being reasonable to judge whether two smells are identical or not. In the bipartite case, the scenario can be interpreted as a natural generalization of full-correlator inequalities (XOR games) beyond binary outputs. We define the sub-polytope of the local polytope corresponding to this scenario and solve it for several bipartite and multipartite scenarios by leveraging some structural properties. In doing so, we obtain thousands of new tight inequalities, many of which are also facets of the standard local polytope. We also define unanimous Bell inequalities, a particular case of the previous class applied to the multipartite setting in which only full-equality events (all outcomes equal) are considered. We show that such inequalities can always be written as deterministic nonlocal games, and we give a simple multipartite unanimous family and prove its local bound. We show that most of these inequalities admit quantum violations, and we also display aspects of their importance for nonlocality. For instance, we identify examples where such inequalities can act as dimension witnesses, outcome witnesses, witnesses of genuine multipartite nonlocality, as well as being relevant to CHSH. These results show that these simple and elegant inequalities by themselves provide a powerful tool for discovering new Bell inequalities and device-independent witnesses.
one_liner: New Bell inequalities based only on equality comparisons generate broad device-independent witnesses even when outcomes are hard to label.
why_it_matters: It broadens experimentally useful nonlocality tests to settings with poorly characterized outputs, relevant for robust device-independent protocols.
detailed_summary: The authors study Bell scenarios where observers only judge whether outcomes are equal, rather than assigning explicit output labels. They characterize the associated local sub-polytope in several bipartite and multipartite settings and derive thousands of tight inequalities, including a unanimous subclass based on full-equality events. Many of these inequalities admit quantum violations and can serve as dimension witnesses, outcome witnesses, witnesses of genuine multipartite nonlocality, and links to CHSH-type structure.
main_result: The paper introduces and solves a large family of equality-based Bell inequalities that retain strong quantum violations and multiple witnessing capabilities.
application_angle: These inequalities could simplify experimental nonlocality certification in quantum communication setups where detector outputs are difficult to calibrate or label precisely.

title: Ultra-Thin Aluminum-Doped Silver for Transmissive Thermally Reconfigurable Visible Photonics
abstract: Functional materials with high electrical conductivity and optical transmittance are vital for thermally tunable free-space photonic systems. Conventional transparent conductors such as graphene and indium tin oxide are limited by high contact resistance, poor mechanical stability, or complex fabrication. Ultra-thin metals, such as pure silver, have also been explored with limited success due to thermal instability and dewetting. Here, we propose an ultra-thin Al-doped Ag film to tackle these challenges. Aluminum promotes heterogeneous nucleation of silver, enabling the formation of continuous, smooth films that are thermally stable at reduced thicknesses while maintaining excellent electrical conductivity and transparency. We find that a 12 nm Al-doped Ag film exhibits an average transmittance of 80% across the visible range with a sheet resistance of 8.3$\pm$1.16 $Ω$cm$^2$. Moreover, on-chip Al-doped Ag microheaters exhibit uniform, rapid thermal response, and stable electrical performance, maintaining functionality for over $10^7$ ON and OFF cycles at temperatures below 400$°$C. Furthermore, as a benchmark, we demonstrate reversible phase-change switching in Ge$_2$Sb$_2$Se$_4$Te (GSST) and VO$_2$. 30$\times$30 $μ$m$^2$ GSST cells exhibited complete crystallization and amorphization under 2.2 V - 200 ms and 4.1V - 50$μ$s pulses, respectively, resulting in a 40% transmission contrast at 780 nm and a tenfold improvement in power consumption compared to similar devices. Additionally, VO$_2$ films displayed reversible insulator-to-metal transitions near 65°C with reflectance and transmittance modulation in the visible and the near-infrared at frequencies up to 25 Hz with room for improvement. These results establish Al-doped Ag as a robust transparent metallic heater for integration in dynamic metasurfaces, optical coatings, and more.
one_liner: Ultra-thin Al-doped Ag films deliver transparent, durable microheaters for visible reconfigurable photonics and phase-change switching.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core materials bottleneck for low-power, high-cycle transmissive photonics by combining transparency, conductivity, and thermal stability in one heater stack.
detailed_summary: The study shows that aluminum doping promotes continuous, smooth, thermally stable ultrathin silver films suitable for transparent heating. A 12 nm Al-doped Ag film reaches about 80% average visible transmittance with sheet resistance of 8.3 plus or minus 1.16 ohms per square and survives more than 10^7 switching cycles below 400 C. Integrated heaters drive reversible switching in GSST and VO2, including complete GSST crystallization and amorphization with 40% transmission contrast at 780 nm and markedly reduced power consumption.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a transparent metallic heater platform that combines high visible transmission, low resistance, long cycling endurance, and effective actuation of phase-change photonic media.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to practical dynamic metasurfaces, tunable optical coatings, and free-space photonic hardware that need scalable low-power visible-range reconfiguration.

title: Attractor-Keyed Memory
abstract: Physical selectors (lasers choosing a mode, Ising machines settling on a ground state, condensates occupying a spin state) produce high-dimensional signatures at the moment of decision: full field amplitudes, multimode interference patterns, or scattering responses. These signatures are richer than the winner's index, yet they are routinely discarded. We show that when the signatures are repeatable across trials (stereotyped) and linearly independent across routes, a single linear decoder compiled from calibration data maps them to arbitrary payloads, merging selection and memory access into one event and eliminating the fetch that dominates latency and energy in sparse routing architectures. The construction requires one SVD of measured device responses, which certifies capability and bounds worst-case error for any downstream payload before the task is chosen. Runtime error separates into two independently diagnosable channels, decoding fidelity (controlled by dictionary conditioning $σ_{\min}(Φ)$) and routing reliability (controlled by the margin-to-noise ratio $Δ/T_{\mathrm{eff}}$), each with a distinct physical origin and targeted remedy. We derive the full error decomposition, give Ising-machine selector constructions, and validate the predicted scalings on synthetic speckle-signature simulations across three measurement modalities. No hardware demonstration exists; we provide a falsifiable four-step experimental protocol specifying what a first experiment must measure. Whether real device signatures satisfy stereotypy is the central open question.
one_liner: Attractor-keyed memory uses rich physical decision signatures to merge selection and memory retrieval through a single learned linear decoder.
why_it_matters: It proposes a hardware-friendly compute-memory primitive that could cut latency and energy in sparse routing systems if real selector signatures prove repeatable enough.
detailed_summary: The paper argues that physical selectors such as lasers, Ising machines, and condensates emit high-dimensional signatures at decision time that can encode more than the winning state index. If these signatures are stereotyped across trials and linearly independent across routes, a single SVD-calibrated linear decoder can map them directly to arbitrary payloads. The authors derive an error decomposition into decoder conditioning and routing reliability, validate expected scaling in synthetic speckle-signature simulations, and define a concrete four-step protocol for a first hardware experiment.
platform_or_system: physical selector systems with high-dimensional signatures
main_result: The main result is a theory and calibration procedure showing how measured attractor signatures can act as direct memory keys, with explicit error bounds and experimental falsifiability.
application_angle: If demonstrated in photonic or Ising-style hardware, this could enable low-latency, low-energy compute-memory fusion for specialized routing and inference architectures.

title: High-Capacity Urban Terrestrial Free-Space Optical Communication Links at km-Scale
abstract: Free-space optical communication links can enable high-capacity wireless connectivity in urban areas. We discuss the feasibility, challenges, and recent developments for high-capacity urban free-space optical links at kilometer scale.
one_liner: A feasibility study outlines kilometer-scale urban free-space optical links for very high-capacity wireless backhaul and access.
why_it_matters: It frames the practical path to deploy city-scale optical wireless links that could complement fiber while informing future quantum-compatible urban optical infrastructure.
detailed_summary: This paper analyzes kilometer-scale terrestrial free-space optical communication in urban environments, emphasizing feasibility, deployment constraints, and recent technical progress. The focus is on high-capacity links and the major practical limitations set by turbulence, alignment, obstruction, and weather-dependent channel loss. Although primarily classical, the discussion is directly relevant to outdoor line-of-sight optical channels that also matter for secure optical networking and future quantum communication deployments.
main_result: The work synthesizes current feasibility and engineering constraints for deploying high-capacity kilometer-scale urban free-space optical links.
application_angle: It is relevant for practical metropolitan last-mile/backhaul connectivity and for designing outdoor optical channels that could later host secure or quantum links.

title: A Lensless Polarization Camera
abstract: Polarization imaging is a technique that creates a pixel map of the polarization state in a scene. Although invisible to the human eye, polarization can assist various sensing and computer vision tasks. Existing polarization cameras use spatial or temporal multiplexing, which increases the camera volume, weight, cost, or all of the above. Recent lensless imaging approaches, such as DiffuserCam, have demonstrated that compact imaging systems can be realized by replacing the lens with a coding element and performing computational reconstruction. In this work, we propose a compact lensless polarization camera composed of a diffuser and a simple striped polarization mask. By combining this optical design with a reconstruction algorithm that explicitly models the polarization-encoded lensless measurements, four linear polarization images are recovered from a single snapshot. Our results demonstrate the potential of lensless approaches for polarization imaging and reveal the physical factors that govern reconstruction quality, guiding the development of high-quality practical systems.
one_liner: A compact lensless camera recovers four linear polarization images from a single snapshot using a diffuser and striped polarization mask.
why_it_matters: It reduces size, weight, and cost for polarization imaging, opening a path to deployable computational sensors in robotics, remote sensing, and machine vision.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a lensless polarization imager that replaces conventional bulk optics with a diffuser and a striped polarization mask. A forward model explicitly incorporates polarization encoding in the lensless measurements, and the reconstruction algorithm recovers four linear polarization components from one exposure. The work also identifies the physical factors controlling reconstruction quality, which is important for optimizing practical compact systems.
main_result: The system demonstrates single-shot recovery of four linear polarization images using a compact lensless optical architecture and computational reconstruction.
application_angle: This could enable low-SWaP polarization sensors for embedded vision, autonomous platforms, and compact scientific or industrial imagers.

title: Cache Hierarchy and Vectorization Analysis of Lindblad Master Equation Simulation for Near-Term Quantum Control
abstract: Simulation of open quantum systems via the Lindblad master equation is a computational bottleneck in near-term quantum control workflows, including optimal pulse engineering (GRAPE), trajectory-based robustness analysis, and feedback controller design. For the system sizes relevant to near-term quantum control ($d = 3$ for a single transmon with leakage, $d = 9$ for two-qubit, and $d = 27$ for three-qubit), the dominant cost per timestep is a $(d^2 \times d^2)$ complex matrix-vector multiplication: a $9\times9$, $81\times81$, or $729\times729$ dense matvec, respectively. The working set sizes (1.5 KB, 105 KB, and 8.1 MB) straddle the L1, L2, and L3 cache boundaries of modern CPUs, making this an ideal system for cache-hierarchy performance analysis. We characterize the arithmetic intensity ($\approx 1/2$ FLOP/byte in the large-$d$ limit), construct a Roofline model for the propagation kernel, and systematically vary compiler flags and data layout to isolate the contributions of auto-vectorization, fused multiply-add, and structure-of-arrays (SoA) memory layout. We show that SoA layout combined with -O3 -march=native -ffast-math yields $2$--$4\times$ speedup over scalar array-of-structures baselines, and that -ffast-math is essential for enabling GCC auto-vectorization of complex arithmetic. These results motivate a set of concrete recommendations for authors of quantum simulation libraries targeting near-term system sizes.
one_liner: A cache- and vectorization-aware study speeds Lindblad master-equation propagation kernels by 2-4x for near-term quantum-control simulations.
why_it_matters: Faster open-system simulation directly improves pulse optimization and feedback design loops for superconducting and other near-term quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper studies dense complex matrix-vector propagation kernels arising in Lindblad master-equation simulation for dimensions d=3, 9, and 27, corresponding to near-term control-relevant systems. By analyzing arithmetic intensity and cache fit across L1, L2, and L3, the authors build a Roofline-based performance model for the kernel. They show that structure-of-arrays layout plus aggressive compiler optimization, especially `-ffast-math`, enables GCC auto-vectorization and produces 2-4x speedups over scalar array-of-structures baselines.
platform_or_system: CPU simulation stack for near-term quantum control
main_result: A structure-of-arrays implementation with optimized compiler settings delivers 2-4x faster Lindblad propagation for control-scale open quantum systems.
application_angle: These results shorten simulation bottlenecks in GRAPE, robustness analysis, and controller design workflows used to tune real quantum devices.

title: Optimizing Logical Mappings for Quantum Low-Density Parity Check Codes
abstract: Early demonstrations of fault tolerant quantum systems have paved the way for logical-level compilation. For fault-tolerant applications to succeed, execution must finish with a low total program error rate (i.e., a low program failure rate). In this work, we study a promising candidate for future fault-tolerant architectures with low spatial overhead: the Gross code. Compilation for the Gross code entails compiling to Pauli Based Computation and then reducing the rotations and measurements to the Bicycle ISA. Depending on the configuration of modules and the placement of code modules on hardware, one can reduce the amount of resulting Bicycle instructions to produce a lower overall error rate. We find that NISQ-based, and existing FTQC mappers are insufficient for mapping logical qubits on Gross code architectures because 1. they do not account for the two-level nature of the logical qubit mapping problem, which separates into code modules with distinct measurements, and 2. they naively account only for length two interactions, whereas Pauli-Products are up to length $n$, where $n$ is the number of logical qubits in the circuit. For these reasons, we introduce a two-stage pipeline that first uses hypergraph partitioning to create in-module clusters, and then executes a priority-based algorithm to efficiently assign clusters onto hardware. We find that our mapping policy reduces the error contribution from inter-module measurements, the largest source of error in the Gross Code, by up to $\sim36\%$ in the best case, with an average reduction of $\sim13\%$. On average, we reduce the failure rates from inter-module measurements by $\sim22\%$ with localized factory availability, and by $\sim17\%$ on grid architectures, allowing hardware developers to be less constrained in developing scalable fault tolerant systems due to software driven reductions in program failure rates.
one_liner: A two-stage mapper for Gross-code architectures cuts inter-module error contributions and lowers logical program failure rates.
why_it_matters: Logical-level mapping is a major practical bottleneck for fault-tolerant systems, and software-level reductions in failure rate can relax hardware constraints.
detailed_summary: This work targets logical compilation for the Gross quantum LDPC code, where placement and module assignment strongly affect fault-tolerant execution cost. The authors argue existing NISQ and FTQC mappers fail because they do not capture the two-level mapping structure and the long-range Pauli-product interactions of the target ISA. Their two-stage pipeline uses hypergraph partitioning for in-module clustering and a priority-based assignment algorithm for hardware placement, reducing the dominant inter-module measurement error contribution by up to about 36% and on average lowering associated failure rates by 17-22% depending on architecture.
main_result: The proposed mapping pipeline reduces inter-module measurement errors by up to ~36% and lowers corresponding program failure rates on Gross-code hardware.
application_angle: Better logical mapping improves the viability of scalable fault-tolerant machines by extracting meaningful error-rate gains from software alone.

title: Spontaneous Polarization Suppression of Exciton-Exciton Annihilation in 3R-Stacked MoS$_2$ Bilayers
abstract: Rapid exciton-exciton annihilation (EEA) in two-dimensional semiconductors limits access to high-density excitonic regimes essential for efficient optoelectronic operation under strong excitation. Here, we show that EEA is suppressed by repulsive dipole-dipole interactions between interlayer excitons polarized by the spontaneous polarization intrinsic to rhombohedral (3R)-stacked MoS$_2$ bilayers. Using ultrafast pump-probe spectroscopy, we measure an EEA rate of $γ_{\rm EEA}=(5.03\pm0.99)\times10^{-3}$ cm$^2$ s$^{-1}$ in 3R bilayers, which is approximately 18.2-fold smaller than that in monolayers and 2.9-fold smaller than that in nonpolar 2H bilayers. Despite the higher exciton diffusivity recently reported for 3R relative to 2H bilayers, the reduced EEA rate in 3R indicates a rate-limited regime governed by the close-encounter annihilation probability rather than diffusion. A rate-limited annihilation model incorporating a dipole-dipole repulsive potential captures the observed ratio $γ_{{\rm EEA},3{\rm R}}/γ_{{\rm EEA},2{\rm H}}\approx0.35$ for an exciton-exciton encounter distance of $\sim$1.3 nm, consistent with the bilayer exciton Bohr radius. These results show that spontaneous polarization in 3R-stacked bilayers suppresses nonlinear excitonic losses and provides a route toward high-density excitonics.
one_liner: 3R-stacked MoS2 bilayers show strongly suppressed exciton-exciton annihilation due to spontaneous-polarization-induced dipolar repulsion.
why_it_matters: Suppressing nonlinear exciton loss is critical for high-density excitonic devices, making this stacking geometry attractive for stronger optoelectronic operation.
detailed_summary: Using ultrafast pump-probe spectroscopy, the authors measure an exciton-exciton annihilation rate of about 5.03e-3 cm^2/s in 3R-stacked MoS2 bilayers. This is roughly 18.2x smaller than in monolayers and 2.9x smaller than in nonpolar 2H bilayers, despite higher diffusivity in 3R material. A rate-limited annihilation model with dipole-dipole repulsion reproduces the observed suppression and points to spontaneous polarization as the mechanism that protects interlayer excitons from close-encounter loss.
main_result: Spontaneous polarization in 3R MoS2 bilayers suppresses exciton-exciton annihilation far below monolayer and 2H-bilayer levels.
application_angle: The result supports high-density excitonic photonic and optoelectronic devices with reduced nonlinear loss under strong excitation.

title: Rydberg-State Hopping in a Wavemeter-Locked Dissipative Time-Crystal System
abstract: Rydberg-state hopping is demonstrated in a wavemeter-locked two-photon rubidium system (Rb D2 probe at 780 nm and 480 nm coupler), enabling rapid and repeatable switching between the 65S1/2 and 63D5/2 states without cavity or frequency-comb stabilization. A Fizeau-interferometer wavemeter provides the error signal for a digital feedback loop that simultaneously stabilizes the coupler and commands discrete Rydberg-state changes. The lock achieves sub MHz frequency stability and acquisition rates up to 6.5 GHz/s (0.4283 GHz engaged in 66 ms), extrapolating to ~0.93 s for a ~6 GHz 65S to 63D transition. Time resolved spectra reveal reemergent dissipative time-crystal oscillations after each hop, with distinct state dependent fundamentals and harmonics. This approach addresses the need for dynamically reconfigurable Rydberg state control for on resonant multi band field detection, while the DTC frequency reconfigurability enables adaptive, low frequency E field sensing in compact, cavity free architectures.
one_liner: A wavemeter-locked rubidium setup rapidly hops between Rydberg states while preserving reemergent dissipative time-crystal oscillations.
why_it_matters: Dynamic, cavity-free retuning of Rydberg states is useful for adaptive electric-field sensing and reconfigurable quantum hardware based on atomic resonances.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a two-photon rubidium scheme with 780 nm probe and 480 nm coupler light to switch between the 65S1/2 and 63D5/2 Rydberg states. A Fizeau-interferometer wavemeter supplies the error signal for digital feedback that both stabilizes the coupler and commands discrete state changes, achieving sub-MHz stability and acquisition rates up to 6.5 GHz/s. After each hop, dissipative time-crystal oscillations reappear with state-dependent fundamental and harmonic content, demonstrating tunable nonlinear dynamics in a compact cavity-free platform.
main_result: The system demonstrates rapid repeatable hopping between Rydberg states with sub-MHz lock stability and reconfigurable dissipative time-crystal response.
application_angle: It provides a practical route to compact adaptive Rydberg sensors for low-frequency electric-field detection and reconfigurable atomic hardware.

title: Efficient and flexible preparation of photonic NOON states in a superconducting system
abstract: The NOON states play a critical role as physical resources in quantum information processing and quantum metrology, yet their preparation efficiency and applicability are often constrained by complicated operational procedures or the requirement for nonlinear interactions. In this paper, we propose an efficient protocol to generate the NOON states within two microwave cavities embedded in a superconducting system, assisted by an auxiliary five-level qudit. The state preparation is accomplished in three steps for an arbitrary photon number $N$ by adjusting only external classical fields, while keeping the qudit-cavity coupling strengths and the qudit level spacings fixed. Based on parameters accessible in superconducting systems, numerical simulations show that the protocol achieves relatively high fidelity for the NOON states preparation even in the presence of parameter fluctuations and decoherence effects. Thus, this protocol may provide a practical approach for preparing the NOON states with current technology. Notably, since nonlinear interactions are not required, the protocol is flexible and has the potential to be applied across various physical systems.
one_liner: Three-step control-only protocol prepares arbitrary-N photonic NOON states in two microwave cavities using a five-level superconducting qudit.
why_it_matters: High-fidelity NOON-state generation without tunable couplings or engineered nonlinearities could simplify deployable quantum-metrology resources in circuit QED.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a deterministic-looking three-step sequence for generating NOON states across two microwave resonators coupled to an auxiliary five-level superconducting qudit. The protocol keeps cavity-qudit couplings and qudit level spacings fixed, requiring only external classical-field control as photon number N is varied. Numerical simulations with realistic decoherence and parameter fluctuations indicate relatively high preparation fidelity for arbitrary-N states. The scheme avoids explicit nonlinear interactions, making it adaptable to existing superconducting cavity platforms and potentially portable to other architectures.
platform_or_system: two microwave cavities with a superconducting five-level qudit
main_result: An arbitrary-photon-number NOON state can be prepared in three externally driven steps with high simulated fidelity under realistic imperfections.
application_angle: Robust cavity NOON-state preparation is directly relevant to quantum-enhanced phase sensing and entanglement resources in superconducting photonic processors.

title: Engineering strong coupling with molecular coatings in optical nanocavities
abstract: Quantum emitters near the surface of silver nanoparticles undergo Rabi oscillations in electronic population dynamics due to strong coupling with near-field multipole modes that are not radiative. Low-frequency nanoparticle dipole modes are radiative but do not couple strong enough to quantum emitters. These features limit the observation of strong coupling. Using macroscopic quantum electrodynamics theory within a Lorentzian pseudo-mode approximation for the non-Markovian interaction kernel, we demonstrate that by coating spherical silver nanoparticles with a thin molecular J-aggregate layer, the resulting core-shell plexciton resonance restructures the local electromagnetic vacuum at dipole-mode frequencies to enable Rabi oscillations for quantum emitters that otherwise would only undergo exponential population decay. Specifically, we show for quantum dot emitters in the near field of silver nanospheres of 20 nm radius, that weak-to-strong coupling crossovers can be induced using 2 nm J-aggregate shells. Our work demonstrates the potential of molecular aggregates to enable deep sub-wavelength structuring of the vacuum field for the observation of coherent quantum dynamics in optical nanocavities.
one_liner: A 2 nm J-aggregate shell around silver nanospheres can push nearby quantum dots from weak to strong coupling in deep-subwavelength nanocavities.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical route to engineer optical vacuum modes so coherent emitter dynamics become observable in plasmonic cavities that normally remain too lossy.
detailed_summary: Using macroscopic quantum electrodynamics with a Lorentzian pseudo-mode treatment of non-Markovian interactions, the authors analyze emitters near silver nanospheres coated by thin molecular J-aggregate layers. The molecular shell hybridizes with the nanoparticle response to form a core-shell plexciton resonance that reshapes the local density of states at radiative dipole-mode frequencies. For a 20 nm silver sphere and a nearby quantum dot, a 2 nm shell is predicted to induce a weak-to-strong-coupling crossover and visible Rabi oscillations where bare spheres give only exponential decay. The result identifies molecular coatings as a compact handle for vacuum engineering in optical nanocavities.
main_result: Thin molecular coatings can restructure plasmonic nanocavity modes enough to enable strong coupling and Rabi oscillations for nearby quantum emitters.
application_angle: The approach could support ultrasmall coherent light-matter interfaces for on-chip nanophotonics, single-photon devices, and quantum-optical sensing.

title: General circuit compilation protocol into partially fault-tolerant quantum computing architecture
abstract: As we are entering an early-FTQC era, circuit execution protocols with logical qubits and certain error-correcting codes are being discussed. Here, we propose a circuit execution protocol for the space-time efficient analog rotation (STAR) architecture. Gate operations within the STAR architecture is based on lattice surgery with surface codes, but it allows direct execution of continuous gates $Rz(θ)$ as non-Clifford gates instead of $T = Rz(π/4)$. $Rz(θ)$ operations involve creation of resource states $|m_θ\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} (|0 \rangle + e^{iθ} |1\rangle ) $ followed by ZZ joint measurements with target logical qubits. While employing $Rz(θ)$ enables more efficient circuit execution, both their creations and joint measurements are probabilistic processes and adopt repeat-until-success (RUS) protocols which are likely to result in considerable time overhead. Our circuit execution protocol aims to reduce such time overhead by parallel trials of resource state creations and more frequent trials of joint measurements. By employing quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) in determining resource state allocations within the space, we successfully make our protocol efficient. Furthermore, we proposed performance estimators given the target circuit and qubit topology. It successfully predicts the time performance within less time than actual simulations do, and helps find the optimal qubit topology to run the target circuits efficiently.
one_liner: A QUBO-based compiler and scheduler reduces repeat-until-success overhead in STAR partial-FTQC architectures with direct logical Rz(theta) gates.
why_it_matters: Efficiently mapping circuits onto early fault-tolerant hardware with probabilistic analog rotations is critical for extracting useful performance from near-term logical machines.
detailed_summary: The work develops a circuit-execution protocol for the STAR architecture, where logical operations are implemented with surface-code lattice surgery but continuous non-Clifford Rz(theta) rotations are applied directly via resource states and ZZ joint measurements. Because both resource-state preparation and joint measurements are probabilistic, the main systems problem is minimizing repeat-until-success latency. The authors use QUBO optimization to allocate resource states spatially and schedule more parallel creation and more frequent measurement attempts. They also introduce performance estimators that predict execution time faster than full simulation and help choose qubit topologies for target circuits.
main_result: A compilation and scheduling protocol using QUBO improves time efficiency for probabilistic logical rotation workflows and provides fast runtime estimation.
application_angle: This is practically relevant for operating early logical quantum processors where layout-aware scheduling determines whether analog non-Clifford gates deliver real throughput gains.

title: Generalized Snell's laws for rough interfaces
abstract: In this paper, we consider the reflection and transmission problem of waves by a rapidly oscillating rough interface that exhibits general mixing properties. Using an asymptotic analysis based on a separation of scales, corresponding to a paraxial (parabolic) scaling regime, we precisely characterize the specular and speckle (diffusive) components of the reflected and transmitted fields. A critically scaled interface is considered, in the sense that the amplitudes of the interface fluctuations and the central wavelength are of the same order. When the correlation length of the interface fluctuations is of the same order as the beam width, random specular components arise in both the reflected and transmitted waves, while no speckle component is observed. Equivalently, the reflected and transmitted fields are essentially confined to the cones formed by the specular components (specular cones) with directions given by the classical Snell's law of reflection and refraction. When the correlation length is smaller than the beam width, a specular homogenization regime emerges. In this case, the rough interface can be approximated by an effective flat interface, yielding deterministic specular reflected and transmitted cones. However, broader cones containing the specular cones appear, within which the wavefields form speckle patterns (speckle cones) whose total energy is of leading order. We provide the two-point correlation functions of these speckle patterns and establish a central-limit-theorem-type result, showing that they can be modeled as Gaussian random fields. These results enable the identification of generalized Snell's laws of reflection and transmission, which depend on an effective scattering operator at the interface.
one_liner: Asymptotic wave theory for rough interfaces derives generalized Snell laws separating specular cones from leading-order speckle transport.
why_it_matters: It provides a predictive framework for scattering through realistic rough optical interfaces, which matters for imaging, nanophotonics, and precision wavefront control.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes reflection and transmission at rapidly oscillating rough interfaces in a paraxial scaling where interface amplitude and wavelength are comparably scaled. It rigorously separates specular and diffusive components and shows that the outcome depends strongly on the roughness correlation length relative to beam width. When the correlation length is beam-scale, reflected and transmitted energy remains in random specular cones without leading-order speckle; for smaller correlation lengths, a homogenized specular response coexists with broader speckle cones carrying leading-order energy. Two-point correlations and a central-limit-type result justify Gaussian modeling of the speckle fields and define effective generalized Snell laws.
platform_or_system: waves at statistically rough optical interfaces
main_result: The authors derive regime-dependent generalized reflection and refraction laws and quantify when rough interfaces produce only specular cones versus dominant speckle cones.
application_angle: The framework can inform design and interpretation of optical systems with unavoidable interface disorder, including scattering-aware photonic components and imaging setups.

title: Quantum Field Approaches to Chemical Systems
abstract: Quantum-matter theory (QMT), based on the Schrödinger or Dirac equations, is firmly established for both intra- and intermolecular interactions. However, there are two key issues with QMT. First, its applicability to large molecular complexes is hindered by the relatively high computational cost of the calculations required to achieve high accuracy. Second, fields are also quantum objects that produce many intriguing effects beyond standard QMT approaches to molecular systems. This review focuses on recent developments in quantum-field theory (QFT) approaches to both covalent and non-covalent interactions for molecules in vacuum and subject to environments such as cavities and solvents. QFT provides a rich playground for novel chemical theories and insights. For example, chemical reactions and van der Waals interactions can be manipulated by cavities, boundaries, and optical excitations; novel interactions emerge when molecules interact with quantized fields; systems with millions of atoms could soon be treated with coarse-grained QFT formalisms; and unexpected scaling laws for atomic and molecular properties can emerge when QFT is applied to sets of chemical systems. This review sets the stage for an exciting QFT-driven path for further development of chemical theory.
one_liner: This review maps how quantum-field methods extend chemical modeling to cavity-controlled reactions, long-range interactions, and scalable coarse-grained molecular theories.
why_it_matters: It connects quantized-field effects to chemically relevant observables, framing routes to engineer reactions and interactions for sensing and materials applications.
detailed_summary: The article reviews how quantum-field-theoretic treatments go beyond standard Schrödinger- or Dirac-based quantum-matter theory for molecular systems. It emphasizes field-mediated modifications of covalent and non-covalent interactions, including cavity- and boundary-controlled chemistry, quantized-field-induced intermolecular forces, and solvent or photonic-environment effects. A second theme is scalability: coarse-grained QFT formalisms may enable treatment of molecular assemblies far larger than conventional high-accuracy electronic-structure methods can handle. The review also highlights emergent scaling laws and new interaction channels that become visible only in field-theoretic descriptions.
platform_or_system: molecules in cavities, solvents, and structured quantum fields
main_result: The review argues that QFT-based chemical models capture cavity- and field-driven phenomena inaccessible or inefficient in conventional quantum-matter treatments.
application_angle: These methods are relevant to chemical sensing and materials control because they point to ways of tuning reactions and intermolecular forces with engineered electromagnetic environments.

title: One Key Good, L Keys Better: List Decoding Meets Quantum Privacy Amplification
abstract: We introduce list privacy amplification (LPA), a relaxation of the final step of quantum key distribution (QKD) in which Alice and Bob extract a list of $L$ candidate keys from a raw string correlated with an eavesdropper Eve, with the guarantee that at least one key is perfectly secret while Eve cannot identify which. This parallels list decoding in error-correcting codes: relaxing unique decoding to list decoding increases the decoding radius; analogously, list extraction increases achievable key length beyond the standard quantum leftover hash lemma (QLHL). Within the abstract cryptography framework, we formalise LPA and prove the \emph{Quantum List Leftover Hash Lemma} (QLLHL): an $L$-list of $\ell$-bit keys can be extracted from an $n$-bit source with smooth min-entropy $k$ iff \[ \ell \le k + \log L - 2\log(1/ε) - 3, \] yielding a tight additive $\log L$ gain over QLHL. This gain arises because the index of the secure key is chosen after hashing and hidden from Eve, effectively contributing $\log L$ bits of entropy. Applying QLLHL to BB84-type QKD, a list size $L = 2^{αn'}$ increases the tolerable phase-error threshold from $h^{-1}(1 - h(e_b))$ to $h^{-1}(1 - h(e_b) + α)$, exceeding the standard $\approx 11\%$ bound for any $α> 0$. We prove tightness via a matching intercept-resend attack, establish composability with Wegman--Carter authentication, and present two constructions: a polynomial inner-product hash over $\mathbb{F}_{2^m}$ and a Toeplitz-based variant, running in $O(nL)$ and $O(nL \log n)$ time.
one_liner: List privacy amplification lets QKD output L candidate keys, gaining log L bits over standard privacy amplification while preserving secrecy of at least one key.
why_it_matters: It relaxes the unique-key requirement to improve QKD key rates and tolerable noise thresholds, potentially extending practical secure links.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces list privacy amplification, where Alice and Bob extract a list of L candidate keys such that at least one is perfectly secret and Eve cannot identify which one. Within abstract cryptography, the authors prove a Quantum List Leftover Hash Lemma showing that the extractable key length increases by an additive log L relative to the usual quantum leftover hash lemma. Applied to BB84-type protocols, this lifts the tolerable phase-error threshold beyond the standard roughly 11% limit for any positive list-rate parameter alpha, and the bound is shown tight via an intercept-resend attack. The work also gives composable authentication and two concrete hash constructions with O(nL) and O(nL log n) complexity.
platform_or_system: BB84-type quantum key distribution with list privacy amplification
main_result: Allowing a list of candidate keys yields a tight additive log L gain in extractable secret length and increases the tolerable error threshold for QKD.
application_angle: This could make quantum communication systems more robust to noise and loss by trading single-key uniqueness for better secure throughput and operating margins.

title: Postselection induced localization and coherence in quantum walks on heterogeneous networks
abstract: Postselection of quantum trajectories is known effectively introduce nonlinearity into dynamics of open quantum systems. We study the effect of such non-linearity in continuous-time quantum walks (CTQWs) on networks with homogeneous and heterogeneous degree distributions. Using the recently proposed nonlinear Lindblad master equation (NLME), we investigate the dynamics under two decoherence mechanisms: Haken-Strobl and quantum stochastic walk (QSW). Our analysis reveals a striking dichotomy: under Haken-Strobl decoherence the nonlinear contributions precisely cancel, yielding a uniform steady state independent of postselection details. In stark contrast, QSW decoherence permits postselection to break dynamical balance on heterogeneous networks, inducing robust localization preferentially at low-degree (peripheral) nodes. Remarkably, this localized state maintains finite quantum coherence. Extending our results to many-body spin systems, we demonstrate that degree heterogeneity similarly stabilizes localization of spin-up excitations in spin-down backgrounds, enhancing entanglement preservation. These findings establish degree heterogeneity and postselection as joint control parameters for engineering quantum transport and localization in dissipative dynamics.
one_liner: Postselection plus network heterogeneity can drive coherent localization in dissipative continuous-time quantum walks.
why_it_matters: It identifies controllable localization and coherence preservation mechanisms that could be exploited in engineered quantum transport and photonic network simulators.
detailed_summary: The paper studies continuous-time quantum walks on networks using a nonlinear Lindblad master equation induced by postselected trajectories. For Haken-Strobl decoherence, the nonlinear terms cancel and the system relaxes to a uniform steady state, but under quantum stochastic walk dynamics on heterogeneous graphs, postselection breaks dynamical balance and localizes population on low-degree nodes. The resulting localized states retain finite coherence, and analogous behavior appears in many-body spin networks where excitation localization improves entanglement preservation. This establishes degree heterogeneity and postselection as tunable handles for dissipative transport engineering.
platform_or_system: Open quantum walks on heterogeneous networks
main_result: Postselection under QSW decoherence induces robust, coherent localization on peripheral nodes of heterogeneous networks, unlike the uniform steady state found for Haken-Strobl noise.
application_angle: The mechanism could be used to design transport, routing, and state-retention behavior in photonic quantum-walk simulators and related dissipative quantum networks.

title: Stability of a high-finesse optical cavity at 493 nm in vacuum for cavity QED with Barium ions
abstract: We explore the stability of a high-finesse optical cavity at 493 nm in vacuum for cavity QED with Barium ions. A high-finesse Fabry-Perot cavity is built using mirrors with high-reflectivity (HR) coatings that are implemented by stacking multiple thin films of low-loss dielectrics on substrates. Applications of such HR mirrors in the near ultraviolet (UV) range have been hampered by degradation of coatings in vacuum. Here, we explore the degradation of mirrors with HR coatings at 493 nm in vacuum. We study both vacuum-induced and laser-induced effects on oxide-coated cavity mirrors by probing changes in cavity loss using cavity lifetime measurements. We investigate the role of circulating power in the rate of increase in cavity loss and demonstrate methods of reversal of cavity degradation. While we observe no degradation without long exposure or with short exposures at lower circulating powers, we find evidence of degradation on long exposure to high circulating powers. We discuss potential causes and conclude that laser-induced deposition is the likely cause while ruling out thermally activated processes due to laser-induced heating.
one_liner: A 493 nm high-finesse cavity in vacuum is characterized for Ba+ cavity QED, including degradation and recovery behavior.
why_it_matters: Reliable near-UV cavities are a bottleneck for trapped-ion cavity QED, and this work isolates operating regimes and likely failure modes for practical deployment.
detailed_summary: The authors build and monitor a high-finesse Fabry-Perot cavity at 493 nm using cavity lifetime measurements to track loss in vacuum. They distinguish vacuum-induced from laser-induced effects and find little degradation for short exposure or lower circulating power, but measurable loss growth after long high-power exposure. Their analysis points to laser-induced deposition on oxide-coated mirrors rather than thermally activated damage, and they also demonstrate reversal methods for the degradation. The study directly addresses the durability of near-UV cavity hardware for Ba+ experiments.
platform_or_system: 493 nm Fabry-Perot cavity for Ba+ ions
main_result: The cavity remains stable in benign operating regimes, while long high-power exposure increases loss consistent with laser-induced deposition rather than heating-driven mirror damage.
application_angle: These results inform operating limits and maintenance strategies for ion-cavity interfaces needed in trapped-ion quantum networking and cavity-QED hardware.

title: Implementation of non-local arbitrary two-qubit controlled gates via geometric quantum computation with Rydberg anti-blockade
abstract: In the context of Rydberg anti-blockade, this paper proposes a new scheme for a high-fidelity controlled-unitary gate based on non-adiabatic holonomic quantum computation. Under specific detuning and interaction conditions, the scheme achieves a suitable evolution path for non-adiabatic holonomic quantum computation through reverse engineering of pulse parameters. Numerical simulations show that the geometric gate maintains high fidelity even in the presence of spontaneous radiation and laser intensity errors. Finally,we extend our designed quantum gates to non-local gates and investigate their use in converting four-qubit entangled states. This finding indicates the potential applicability of our scheme to complex quantum information processing tasks.
one_liner: A Rydberg anti-blockade scheme implements arbitrary controlled two-qubit gates through non-adiabatic holonomic evolution.
why_it_matters: It offers a route to robust, high-fidelity entangling gates with geometric protection and extends naturally to non-local quantum processing tasks.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a non-adiabatic holonomic controlled-unitary gate protocol in the Rydberg anti-blockade regime, using reverse-engineered pulses to generate the desired geometric evolution path. Under specific detuning and interaction settings, the scheme realizes arbitrary controlled two-qubit operations while preserving the geometric character of the gate. Numerical simulations indicate high fidelity in the presence of spontaneous emission and laser intensity errors. The authors further extend the construction to non-local gates and apply it to transformations among four-qubit entangled states.
main_result: The scheme realizes high-fidelity arbitrary controlled two-qubit and non-local gates via non-adiabatic holonomic control in a Rydberg platform.
application_angle: It is relevant for scalable neutral-atom processors where robust geometric entangling gates can improve circuit reliability and distributed gate design.

title: Design and implementation of a modular laser system for AMO experiments
abstract: Robust laser delivery and stabilization are key components in atom-based quantum technologies, such as quantum computing. Moving these technologies towards product-like deployment requires scalable, compact, cost-effective, and upgradable modules. Here we describe laser systems consisting of application-flexible modules, and demonstrate their performance by characterizing key metrics and by integration with ion trap systems. The laser system is confined to a single server rack and a compact locking station. Both are Class 1 laser products with fiber in-out and electronic control of the laser light. This is achieved through precision manufacture of optical boards that are designed to reduce the degrees of freedom, ease alignment, and increase the robustness to environmental factors. We present a range of 13 wavelengths from 375 nm to 1092 nm: efficiencies from laser source to ion trap range from 21 - 28%, with laser stabilization line widths below 1 MHz.
one_liner: A rack-scale modular laser stack delivers 13 stabilized wavelengths for AMO and ion-trap quantum technology experiments.
why_it_matters: Integrated laser infrastructure is essential for moving trapped-ion and AMO systems from lab setups toward scalable deployable hardware.
detailed_summary: The work presents a modular laser architecture packaged into a single server rack plus a compact locking station, with fiber input/output and electronic control. It spans 13 wavelengths from 375 nm to 1092 nm, achieving source-to-ion-trap efficiencies of 21-28% and sub-megahertz stabilized linewidths. The optical boards are manufactured to reduce alignment complexity and environmental sensitivity, and the complete system is demonstrated in integration with ion-trap experiments. The emphasis is on robustness, compactness, upgradeability, and product-like operation.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a compact Class 1 modular laser system providing 13 stabilized wavelengths with below-1 MHz linewidths and practical coupling efficiencies for ion-trap use.
application_angle: This kind of integrated photonic control hardware reduces system complexity and is directly useful for scaling trapped-ion quantum computers and related AMO instruments.

title: Two-Step Tapering-Collapse Method Enables Element-Interdiffused Cladding for Enhanced Laser Amplification in Yb:YAG Single Crystal Fibers
abstract: The development of high-power single crystal fiber (SCF) lasers is critically hindered by the lack of a reliable cladding scheme to confine the optical mode and ensure beam quality. Here, we propose and demonstrate a two-step tapering-collapse method for the first time to fabricate a high-quality cladding on Yb:YAG SCFs based on elemental interdiffusion. This in-situ formed crystalline transition layer with a graded refractive index effectively suppresses lattice mismatch and abruptly mitigates core-cladding interfacial stress. Consequently, the numerical aperture of the SCF is significantly reduced from 0.280 to 0.199. In a master oscillator power amplifier configuration, the clad SCF delivers a remarkable 46.7% enhancement in slope efficiency compared to its bare counterpart, accompanied by a substantially improved near-field beam profile. This work establishes a facile and effective route to high-performance clad SCFs, unlocking their full potential for next-generation extreme-condition lasers.
one_liner: An interdiffused cladding process improves confinement and amplifier efficiency in Yb:YAG single-crystal fibers.
why_it_matters: Better beam quality and efficiency in crystalline fiber gain media directly support high-power laser systems used in precision sensing and extreme-environment applications.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a two-step tapering-collapse fabrication route that forms an element-interdiffused cladding on Yb:YAG single-crystal fibers. The resulting graded-index crystalline transition layer suppresses lattice mismatch and reduces interfacial stress, lowering the numerical aperture from 0.280 to 0.199. In a master-oscillator power-amplifier configuration, the clad fiber shows a 46.7% slope-efficiency increase relative to an unclad fiber and yields a cleaner near-field mode profile. The method provides a practical path to higher-performance crystalline fiber amplifiers.
main_result: The interdiffused clad single-crystal fiber delivers markedly improved mode confinement, better beam profile, and a 46.7% enhancement in slope efficiency.
application_angle: The fabrication strategy can enable more efficient high-power laser sources for demanding sensing, metrology, and industrial photonics environments.

title: Reconfigurable circuit for mode tunable topological quantum structured light
abstract: Structured light in the quantum regime has garnered considerable attention due to the opportunities it offers when mixing light's internal degrees of freedom, for high-dimensional and multi-dimensional quantum states of light. A popular example is to harness polarisation and spatial entangled photons with a shared topological invariant that is robust against numerous families of noisy quantum channels. Yet, producing such states with high purity and adaptability remains challenging. Here we introduce a compact, self-locking Mach-Zehnder interferometer that integrates digital spatial light modulators with static beam displacers to map spatial-mode entanglement from a parametric down-conversion source onto topological entanglement with high fidelity. The device also mimics the action of a reprogrammable controlled-unitary gate, digitally driven by the spatial light modulator. This approach is an enabling platform and provides a practical route to generating reliable, high-purity quantum-structured light with topological features, both at the single-photon level and as entangled states, a direction of growing topical interest.
one_liner: A self-locking interferometric circuit generates tunable topological quantum structured light with high fidelity.
why_it_matters: Reconfigurable generation of robust high-dimensional photonic entanglement is valuable for quantum communications, photonic processing, and noise-resilient state engineering.
detailed_summary: This work develops a compact self-locking Mach-Zehnder interferometer that combines digital spatial light modulators with static beam displacers. The circuit maps spatial-mode entanglement from an SPDC source into topological entanglement with high fidelity and can be reprogrammed to tune the output mode structure. Functionally, the setup also emulates a controlled-unitary gate driven by the SLM settings. The platform supports both single-photon and entangled-state generation of structured light with topological protection features.
main_result: The device produces high-purity, mode-tunable topological quantum structured light and reconfigurably maps SPDC spatial entanglement into topological entanglement.
application_angle: It is a practical source and processing primitive for high-dimensional photonic links and robust structured-light protocols in quantum communication.

title: Fast stabilizer state preparation via AI-optimized graph decimation
abstract: We propose a general method for preparing stabilizer states with reduced two-qubit gate count and depth compared to the state of the art. The method starts from a graph state representation of the stabilizer state and iteratively reduces the number of edges in the graph using two-qubit Clifford gates to produce a unitary preparation circuit. We explore various heuristic search and AI-based approaches to optimally choose Clifford gates at each step, the most sophisticated of which is a combination of reinforcement learning and Monte Carlo tree search that we call QuSynth. We apply our method to synthesize code states of various quantum error correcting codes including the 23-qubit Golay code and the 144-qubit gross code, the latter of which is significantly beyond the qubit number that is accessible to prior optimal circuit synthesis methods. We demonstrate that our techniques are capable of reducing the required two-qubit gates by up to a factor of 2.5 compared to previous approaches while retaining low circuit depth.
one_liner: An AI-guided graph-decimation method synthesizes stabilizer-state circuits with far fewer two-qubit gates while keeping depth low.
