why_it_matters: Lower entangling-gate count directly improves fidelity and makes large code-state preparation more practical on noisy quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper maps stabilizer-state preparation to iterative edge removal on a graph-state representation, where each step applies a two-qubit Clifford operation chosen to simplify the graph. It develops heuristic and AI-driven search strategies, culminating in QuSynth, a reinforcement-learning plus Monte Carlo tree search method for circuit synthesis. On code states including the 23-qubit Golay code and the 144-qubit gross code, the method reaches system sizes beyond prior optimal synthesis approaches while cutting two-qubit gate counts by up to 2.5x and preserving low depth.
platform_or_system: stabilizer-state circuits for superconducting or generic qubit hardware
main_result: QuSynth produces low-depth stabilizer preparation circuits with up to 2.5x fewer two-qubit gates than prior methods, including for very large code states.
application_angle: The approach can reduce overhead for initializing quantum error-correcting code states on near-term hardware where entangling gates dominate error budgets.

title: Isotopic variations and Zeeman-like splitting in the spectra of nonlinear photonic meta-atoms
abstract: We study photonic meta-atoms, a unique class of composite solitary wave, supported in nonlinear waveguides. We establish an analogy to one-dimensional soft-core atoms, allowing to describe the complex dynamics via concepts from atomic physics. Higher-order dispersive effects cause specific spectral resonances characteristic for the eigenspectrum of a meta-atom. We demonstrate that subtle changes in this level spectrum causes frequency shifts of the resonances. These shifts consist of isotopic and isomeric contributions that can be distinguished in terms of a simple model. We further demonstrate a generic mechanism that causes a Zeeman-like splitting of resonance lines.
one_liner: Nonlinear photonic meta-atoms show isotope-like and Zeeman-like spectral shifts, extending atomic-style level-structure concepts to solitary-wave optics.
why_it_matters: It provides a physics-based framework for predicting and tuning resonances in nonlinear photonic structures used for spectral control and wave-based devices.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze composite solitary waves in nonlinear waveguides as photonic meta-atoms with an effective soft-core atomic analogy. Higher-order dispersion generates discrete spectral resonances tied to the meta-atom eigenspectrum, and small structural variations produce separable isotopic and isomeric frequency shifts. They further identify a generic mechanism that splits resonance lines in a Zeeman-like manner, giving a compact model for interpreting the resulting spectra.
main_result: The work predicts and models isotope-like, isomeric, and Zeeman-like resonance shifts in nonlinear photonic meta-atoms.
application_angle: These spectral signatures could be exploited to engineer tunable nonlinear optical responses and precision resonance control in integrated photonic devices.

title: A Continuous-Variable Quantum Fourier Layer: Applications to Filtering and PDE Solving
abstract: Fourier representations play a central role in operator learning methods for partial differential equations and are increasingly being explored in quantum machine learning architectures. The classical fast Fourier transform (FFT), particularly in its Cooley--Tukey decomposition, exhibits a structure that naturally matches continuous-variable quantum circuits. This correspondence establishes a direct structural isomorphism between the Cooley-Tukey butterfly network and Gaussian photonic gates, enabling the FFT to be realized as a native optical computation in continuous-variable quantum computing. Building on this observation, we introduce a continuous-variable Quantum Fourier Layer (CV--QFL) based on a bipartite Gaussian encoding and a Cooley-Tukey quantum Fourier transform, enabling exact two-dimensional spectral processing within a Gaussian photonic circuit. We test the CV--QFL on two representative tasks: spectral low-pass filtering and Fourier-domain integration of the heat equation. In both cases, the results match the classical reference to machine precision. Beyond these examples, our method naturally extends to optical-input settings in which the signal is already available as a Gaussian optical field. In such scenarios, coherent light coupled into single-mode waveguides can be processed directly by the CV--QFL, bypassing the need for an explicit classical-to-quantum encoding stage. This enables native spectral processing of light and lays the groundwork for new approaches to quantum scientific machine learning, in particular for future neural operator architectures within the CV framework.
one_liner: A continuous-variable quantum Fourier layer implements exact 2D spectral processing in Gaussian photonic circuits for filtering and PDE solving.
why_it_matters: It shows that useful Fourier-domain computations can be native to photonic CV hardware, avoiding approximation error and potentially reducing encoding overhead.
detailed_summary: The paper exploits a structural isomorphism between the Cooley-Tukey FFT butterfly network and Gaussian photonic gate decompositions to define a continuous-variable Quantum Fourier Layer. Using a bipartite Gaussian encoding and a CV quantum Fourier transform, the layer performs exact two-dimensional spectral processing inside a Gaussian circuit. Simulations on low-pass filtering and Fourier-domain integration of the heat equation match classical references to machine precision, and the authors argue that optical input fields could be processed directly without explicit classical-to-quantum encoding.
main_result: The proposed CV-QFL exactly reproduces 2D Fourier-domain filtering and heat-equation integration with machine-precision agreement to classical results.
application_angle: This can serve as a native optical primitive for scientific machine learning, neural operators, and real-time spectral processing of already-optical signals.

title: Reconfigurable Resonant Multimode Nonlinear Coupling for UV-to-infrared Frequency Generation
abstract: On-chip coherent visible and near-infrared (NIR) light generation has broad applications in metrology, bio-sensing, and quantum information. High-Q microresonators are ideal candidates for generating light across such broad wavelength ranges via efficient second- ($χ^{(2)}$) and third-order ($χ^{(3)}$) nonlinear optical processes. However, harnessing these diverse nonlinearities simultaneously in a single microresonator remains elusive yet highly attractive both fundamentally and technologically. Here, we demonstrate coherent light generation from the ultraviolet to NIR in a silicon nitride microresonator pumped by a single continuous-wave telecom laser. This broad frequency generation arises from the interplay of $χ^{(2)}$ and $χ^{(3)}$ nonlinear processes. A cascade of nonlinear processes, including harmonic generation and optical parametric oscillation (OPO), is initiated by the photoinduced second harmonic generation enabled by all-optical poling. The dynamic reconfigurability of this $χ^{(2)}$ nonlinearity enables access to different transverse spatial modes at the second harmonic, enabling highly tunable OPO processes triggered by hybrid modal phase matching conditions and yielding milliwatt-level NIR light. This work sheds new insights into the fundamental physics of cooperative nonlinear multimode interactions in resonant systems and provides a versatile approach for reconfigurable OPOs, highlighting their potential to generate light at wavelengths beyond the reach of photonic integrated lasers.
one_liner: A single telecom pump drives silicon nitride microresonators to generate coherent UV-to-NIR light through reconfigurable chi(2)-chi(3) interactions.
why_it_matters: Broadband, tunable on-chip frequency generation from one pump laser is valuable for compact metrology, sensing, and quantum photonic sources.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate ultraviolet to near-infrared coherent light generation in a high-Q silicon nitride microresonator pumped by a continuous-wave telecom laser. Photoinduced second-harmonic generation created by all-optical poling seeds a cascade involving harmonic generation and optical parametric oscillation, with both chi(2) and chi(3) processes participating. Because the induced chi(2) response is dynamically reconfigurable, different second-harmonic transverse modes can be accessed to realize hybrid modal phase matching and tunable OPO output, including milliwatt-level NIR generation.
main_result: The device generates coherent UV-to-NIR output from a single telecom pump via reconfigurable multimode chi(2)-chi(3) nonlinear dynamics.
application_angle: It offers a practical route to integrated wavelength-agile light sources for spectroscopy, biosensing, precision metrology, and quantum photonic interfaces.

title: Zeno and anti-Zeno effects in dark-state dynamics under thermal dephasing
abstract: The quantum Zeno and anti-Zeno effects describe how frequent measurements can either suppress or accelerate quantum dynamics. While extensively studied in various platforms, their manifestation in dark-state dynamics remains largely unexplored. Here we investigate the stability of dark states in a cavity QED system consisting of two atoms coupled to a single-mode cavity, subject to thermal dephasing that models continuous quantum non-demolition monitoring. Using the Tavis--Cummings model within a Lindblad master equation framework, we numerically analyze how measurement-induced dephasing affects dark-state retention and stabilization time. We identify distinct parameter regimes corresponding to Zeno and anti-Zeno behavior: at low dephasing intensities, increasing the measurement strength accelerates the loss of dark-state coherence (anti-Zeno regime), while at higher intensities, it slows down the dynamics and partially recovers dark-state weight (Zeno regime). The transition between these regimes is controlled by the dephasing rates, the cavity photon exchange, and the asymmetry in atom-field couplings. We show that even under strong dephasing, a finite dark-state component persists, demonstrating remarkable robustness. Our results provide insights into the interplay between measurement back-action and decoherence in open quantum systems, with implications for quantum control and information storage.
one_liner: Thermal-dephasing-driven monitoring in cavity QED produces both Zeno and anti-Zeno regimes in dark-state dynamics, with residual dark-state robustness.
why_it_matters: Understanding when measurement back-action protects or destabilizes dark states is important for quantum memories, control protocols, and decoherence management.
detailed_summary: Using a Tavis-Cummings cavity QED model with Lindblad dephasing, the paper studies two atoms coupled to a single cavity mode under thermal dephasing interpreted as continuous QND monitoring. Numerical analysis shows that weak dephasing can accelerate dark-state decay, producing an anti-Zeno regime, while stronger dephasing slows the dynamics and partially restores dark-state population, indicating Zeno behavior. The crossover depends on dephasing strength, photon-exchange rate, and atom-cavity coupling asymmetry, and a finite dark-state component survives even under strong dephasing.
main_result: The study identifies parameter-controlled transitions between anti-Zeno and Zeno behavior in monitored dark-state dynamics, with persistent dark-state weight at strong dephasing.
application_angle: These results inform strategies for stabilizing protected states in cavity-based quantum memories and measurement-assisted control architectures.

title: Exploration of Fluxonium Parameters for Capacitive Cross-Resonance Gates
abstract: We study the cross-resonance effect in capacitively-coupled fluxonium qubits and devise a simple formula for their maximum ZX interaction strength. By going beyond the perturbative regime, we find that a CNOT gate can generally be realized in under 200 ns with residual ZZ limited to 50 kHz, for fluxonium qubits with frequencies below 1 GHz. Our analysis relies on a semi-analytical method: we first numerically diagonalize the Floquet Hamiltonian of the strongly-driven control qubit and then perturbatively incorporate the weak qubit-qubit coupling to obtain an effective Hamiltonian. We also derive frequency collision windows around harmful control-target and control-spectator transitions. For large fluxonium devices, we predict a collision-free yield that is considerably less sensitive to junction variability compared to transmons in the same layout. These results support the viability of an all-fluxonium cross-resonance architecture with only capacitive couplings.
one_liner: Capacitively coupled fluxonium qubits can realize cross-resonance CNOTs below 200 ns with low residual ZZ and improved collision tolerance.
why_it_matters: Fast, low-crosstalk entangling gates in fluxonium strengthen the case for scalable architectures beyond transmons.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes cross-resonance interactions in capacitively coupled fluxonium qubits and derives a simple estimate for the maximum achievable ZX rate. A semi-analytical method combines numerical diagonalization of the strongly driven control-qubit Floquet Hamiltonian with perturbative treatment of weak inter-qubit coupling to extract effective gate dynamics. Across parameter space with qubit frequencies below 1 GHz, the authors find CNOT implementations generally under 200 ns with residual ZZ around 50 kHz, and they map collision windows for harmful control-target and spectator transitions. For larger devices, the predicted collision-free yield is less sensitive to junction variability than comparable transmon layouts.
main_result: The paper predicts sub-200 ns cross-resonance CNOT gates with residual ZZ limited to about 50 kHz in low-frequency fluxonium systems.
application_angle: This supports an all-fluxonium capacitive architecture that could deliver faster, more fabrication-robust entangling gates for superconducting quantum processors.

title: Beyond VQE and QPE: A Noise- and Sampling-Error-Tolerant Quantum Algorithm with Heisenberg-Limited Precision
abstract: This paper introduces Witnessed Quantum Time Evolution (WQTE), a novel quantum algorithm for efficiently computing the eigen-energy spectra of arbitrary quantum systems without requiring eigenstate preparation-a key limitation of conventional approaches. By leveraging a single ancillary qubit to control real-time evolution operators and employing Fourier analysis, WQTE enables parallel resolution of multiple eigen-energies. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the algorithm achieves Heisenberg-limited precision and operates with only a non-zero wavefunction overlap between the reference state and target eigenstates, significantly reducing initialization complexity. Numerical simulations validate the algorithm's effectiveness in molecular systems (e.g., H4 chains) and lattice models (e.g., Heisenberg spin systems), confirming that computational error scales inversely with maximum evolution time while maintaining robustness against sampling errors and quantum noise. Experimental implementation on an NMR quantum processor further verifies its feasibility in real-world noisy environments. Compared to existing quantum algorithms (e.g., VQE, QPE and their variants), WQTE exhibits superior circuit depth efficiency, resource economy, and noise resilience, making it a promising solution for eigen-energy computation on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices.
one_liner: WQTE estimates multiple eigen-energies on NISQ hardware without eigenstate preparation while retaining Heisenberg-limited precision.
why_it_matters: It offers a more noise-tolerant and shallower route to spectral estimation than VQE or standard QPE, directly relevant to near-term quantum simulation.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces Witnessed Quantum Time Evolution, which uses a single ancilla-controlled real-time evolution and Fourier analysis to resolve several eigen-energies in parallel from a reference state with nonzero overlap. The authors show the estimation error scales inversely with the maximum evolution time, achieving Heisenberg-limited precision while tolerating sampling error and device noise. Numerical studies on H4 chains and Heisenberg spin models support the scaling claims, and an NMR processor experiment demonstrates practical operation under noisy conditions.
main_result: The work demonstrates a noise- and sampling-error-tolerant spectral algorithm that extracts eigen-energies with Heisenberg-limited precision and reduced state-preparation overhead.
application_angle: This could make chemistry and materials energy-spectrum estimation more feasible on shallow, imperfect quantum processors.

title: On the power of multipartite entanglement for pseudotelepathy
abstract: As early as 1935, Schrödinger recognized entanglement as ``not one but rather the characteristic trait of quantum mechanics, the one that enforces its entire departure from classical lines of thought''. Indeed, most remarkable phenomena in quantum information science, such as quantum computing and quantum teleportation, spring from clever uses of entanglement. Among them, pseudotelepathy enables two or more players to win systematically at some cooperative games with no need for communication between them, a restriction that would make the task impossible in a classical world. We investigate the power of multipartite entanglement for pseudotelepathy. Some known games that can be won with tripartite entanglement cannot be won with bipartite entanglement, but they can be won with bipartite nonsignalling resources such as the so-called Popescu--Rohrlich nonlocal box. We exhibit a five-player game that can be won with tripartite entanglement, but not with arbitrary bipartite nonsignalling resources even in the presence of arbitrary five-partite classical resources. This illustrates both the power of bipartite nonsignalling resources (over bipartite entanglement) and the even superior power of tripartite entanglement.
one_liner: A five-player game shows tripartite entanglement enables pseudotelepathy beyond what any bipartite nonsignalling resources can achieve.
why_it_matters: It sharpens the operational advantage of genuine multipartite entanglement, informing distributed quantum-network protocols and communication complexity tasks.
detailed_summary: The authors construct a five-player cooperative game that is perfectly winnable using tripartite entanglement. They prove the same task cannot be won using arbitrary bipartite nonsignalling resources, even when supplemented with arbitrary five-partite classical resources. This separates the power of tripartite entanglement from both bipartite entanglement and stronger-than-quantum bipartite correlations in a communication-free setting.
main_result: The main result is a strict resource separation showing tripartite entanglement can outperform all bipartite nonsignalling-assisted strategies in pseudotelepathy.
application_angle: The result identifies tasks where genuinely multipartite quantum links provide a unique functional advantage for distributed communication and verification protocols.

title: MQTE: A Measurement-Based Quantum Algorithm for Robust Energy Spectrum Estimation in the NISQ Era
abstract: Extracting energy spectra from quantum Hamiltonians is a fundamental task for quantum simulation, yet remains challenging on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. We propose Measured Quantum Time Evolution (MQTE), an ancilla-free algorithm that estimates energy gaps by applying real-time evolution to a reference state and measuring time-resolved probabilities via repeated projective measurements. Spectral analysis of these signals reveals oscillation frequencies corresponding to eigenvalue differences. Crucially, MQTE exhibits inherent robustness to quantum hardware noise and sampling errors: these disturbances manifest as a white-noise background, which does not distort the underlying spectral structure but rather obscures the frequency information. By increasing the number of measurement samples, the intensity of the background white noise can be suppressed, thereby recovering the original spectral content. We validate the algorithm's performance via numerical simulations on one- and two-dimensional Heisenberg models, demonstrating accurate extraction of energy gaps and resilience against both sampling and circuit-level noise. Experimental implementation on the superconducting quantum processor Tianyan-176-II further confirms the practical feasibility and noise tolerance of MQTE under real hardware conditions. This work provides a robust and scalable framework for quantum spectral estimation in the NISQ era.
one_liner: MQTE extracts Hamiltonian energy gaps from time-domain projective measurements without ancillas and remains robust under NISQ noise.
why_it_matters: It provides a hardware-friendly spectrum-estimation primitive whose error can be reduced by sampling rather than deeper coherent circuits.
detailed_summary: Measured Quantum Time Evolution estimates spectra by evolving a reference state in real time and recording time-resolved projective measurement probabilities, whose oscillation frequencies encode eigenvalue differences. The central claim is that hardware noise and sampling errors mainly add a white-noise background instead of biasing the spectral peaks, so increased sampling can recover the relevant frequencies. Simulations on 1D and 2D Heisenberg models and an implementation on Tianyan-176-II validate accurate gap extraction under realistic noise.
main_result: The paper demonstrates ancilla-free, noise-tolerant extraction of Hamiltonian energy gaps from time-series measurement data on NISQ hardware.
application_angle: This is directly useful for near-term quantum simulation workflows where coherence is limited but repeated measurements are affordable.

title: Adaptive Loss-tolerant Syndrome Measurements
abstract: In the presence of qubit losses, the building blocks of fault-tolerant error correction (FTEC) must be revisited. Existing loss-tolerant approaches are mainly architecture-specific, and little attention has been given to optimizing the syndrome measurement sequences under loss. Schemes designed for the standard Pauli error model are not directly applicable because the syndrome patterns differ when both Pauli errors and erasures can occur. Based on recent advances in loss detection units and loss-tolerant syndrome extraction gadgets, we extend the study of adaptive Shor-style measurement sequences to the mixed error model. We begin by discussing how to adaptively convert correctable erasures into located errors. The minimal overhead is quantified by the number of stabilizer measurements, which can be reduced to a subgroup dimension problem for erasures arising in any FTEC circuit for qubits and prime-dimensional qudits. As a byproduct, we provide the construction of the canonical generating set with respect to a given bipartite partition for a stabilizer group on qudits of composite dimension. We then generalize both the weak and strong FTEC conditions. Finally, we present adaptive syndrome-measurement protocols for the mixed error model, generalizing the adaptive protocols for the standard Pauli error model.
one_liner: Adaptive syndrome protocols reduce stabilizer-measurement overhead and handle mixed Pauli-error plus qubit-loss models for fault tolerance.
why_it_matters: Loss is a major practical failure mode in several hardware platforms, so better syndrome extraction directly strengthens realistic fault-tolerant architectures.
detailed_summary: This work extends adaptive Shor-style syndrome measurement to mixed error models containing both Pauli faults and erasures from qubit loss. It shows how to adaptively convert correctable erasures into located errors and frames minimal measurement overhead as a subgroup-dimension problem for stabilizer measurements on qubits and prime-dimensional qudits. The paper also generalizes weak and strong fault-tolerance conditions and provides adaptive protocols tailored to loss-aware syndrome extraction.
main_result: The main result is a generalized adaptive syndrome-measurement framework that explicitly incorporates qubit loss while lowering stabilizer-measurement overhead.
application_angle: The methods are relevant to hardware with significant loss or erasure events, such as photonic and other non-ideal fault-tolerant quantum computing platforms.

title: Towards sample-optimal learning of bosonic Gaussian quantum states
abstract: Continuous-variable systems enable key quantum technologies in computation, communication, and sensing. Bosonic Gaussian states emerge naturally in various such applications, including gravitational-wave and dark-matter detection. A fundamental question is how to characterize an unknown bosonic Gaussian state from as few samples as possible. Despite decades-long exploration, the ultimate efficiency limit remains unclear. In this work, we study the necessary and sufficient number of copies to learn an $n$-mode Gaussian state, with energy less than $E$, to $\varepsilon$ trace distance with high probability. We prove a lower bound of $Ω(n^3/\varepsilon^2)$ for Gaussian measurements, matching the best known upper bound up to doubly-log energy dependence, and $Ω(n^2/\varepsilon^2)$ for arbitrary measurements. We further show an upper bound of $\widetilde{O}(n^2/\varepsilon^2)$ given that the Gaussian state is promised to be either pure or passive. Interestingly, while Gaussian measurements suffice for nearly optimal learning of pure Gaussian states, non-Gaussian measurements are provably required for optimal learning of passive Gaussian states. Finally, focusing on learning single-mode Gaussian states via non-entangling Gaussian measurements, we provide a nearly tight bound of $\widetildeΘ(E/\varepsilon^2)$ for any non-adaptive schemes, showing adaptivity is indispensable for nearly energy-independent scaling. As a byproduct, we establish sharp bounds on the trace distance between Gaussian states in terms of the total variation distance between their Wigner distributions, and obtain a nearly tight sample complexity bound for learning the Wigner distribution of any Gaussian state to $\varepsilon$ total variation distance. Our results greatly advance quantum learning theory in the bosonic regimes and have practical impact in quantum sensing and benchmarking applications.
one_liner: This paper derives near-optimal copy complexity bounds for learning bosonic Gaussian states under Gaussian and general measurements.
why_it_matters: It sets practical efficiency limits for characterizing continuous-variable states used in sensing, benchmarking, and precision measurement.
detailed_summary: The authors establish lower and upper bounds for learning an n-mode Gaussian state of bounded energy to trace-distance error epsilon, including an Ω(n^3/epsilon^2) lower bound for Gaussian measurements and an Ω(n^2/epsilon^2) lower bound for arbitrary measurements. They further show a near-optimal O~(n^2/epsilon^2) upper bound when the state is promised pure or passive, and prove non-Gaussian measurements are necessary for optimal learning of passive Gaussian states. For single-mode settings, they derive nearly tight O~(E/epsilon^2) bounds for non-adaptive non-entangling Gaussian measurements, highlighting the importance of adaptivity.
main_result: The paper identifies nearly tight sample-complexity limits for tomography and learning of Gaussian states, including regimes where non-Gaussian measurements or adaptivity are provably necessary.
application_angle: These bounds guide the design of efficient calibration and readout schemes for CV sensing platforms, including gravitational-wave and dark-matter detection setups.

title: Theory of Two-Qubit $T_2$ Spectroscopy of Quantum Many-Body Systems
abstract: Multi-qubit quantum sensors are rapidly emerging as platforms that extend the capabilities of conventional single-qubit sensing. In this work we show how suitable pulse sequences applied to a two-qubit sensor enable separate extraction of the response and noise of a probed environment within a $T_2$ spectroscopy framework. By resorting to representative examples, we demonstrate that this approach can resolve the spatio-temporal spreading of correlations in a many-body system. In particular, the resulting correlated dephasing signal captures features such as the dispersion of low-energy excitations, which manifest as light-cone-like profiles in the propagation of correlations. We further show that non-equilibrium conditions, for instance those induced by external driving, can modify this profile by producing additional fringes outside the light-cone. As a complementary application, we demonstrate that the method clearly distinguishes between different transport regimes in the system, including ballistic spreading, diffusive broadening, and the crossover between them.
one_liner: Two-qubit T2 spectroscopy can separately recover environmental response and noise while imaging correlation spreading in many-body systems.
why_it_matters: It upgrades quantum sensing from local noise probes to spatially resolved diagnostics of transport, dispersion, and driven many-body dynamics.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a theory in which tailored pulse sequences on a two-qubit sensor isolate distinct response and noise contributions from a coupled many-body environment. The resulting correlated dephasing signal reveals spatio-temporal correlation spreading, including light-cone-like structures associated with low-energy excitation dispersion. The framework also predicts signatures of external driving through fringes outside the light cone and distinguishes ballistic, diffusive, and crossover transport regimes.
platform_or_system: two-qubit quantum sensor coupled to a many-body environment
main_result: The main result is a sensing protocol and theory showing that two-qubit T2 measurements can resolve both dynamics and transport character of a quantum many-body system.
application_angle: This provides a practical route to map non-equilibrium dynamics and transport in condensed-matter or simulator platforms using compact multi-qubit probes.

title: High-dimensional quantum communication with scalable photonic entanglement in time and frequency
abstract: High-dimensional photonic entanglement holds significant promise for advancing quantum communication, computation, and metrology. For example, large-alphabet quantum communication protocols are known to benefit from enhanced noise resilience and information capacity via multi-bit time-bin encoding. Yet, characterizing high-dimensional entangled states is challenging, as full state tomography becomes prohibitively costly and often requires unrealizable measurements. Here, we demonstrate a scan-free method to characterize high-dimensional entanglement in the time-frequency domain. Our reconstruction achieves a record $5.70\pm0.07$ ebits and a fidelity of $65.4\pm0.4\%$ with the maximally entangled state of local dimension $1021$, certifying the presence of $668$-dimensional entanglement. We further prove the attainability of a secure key rate of $15.6$ kB/s in a composable finite-size, entanglement-based protocol, and show that in continuous operation, the setup can quickly approach asymptotic key rates. Using commercial telecom components and state-of-the-art low-jitter single-photon detectors, our scalable architecture offers a practical path towards high-rate, noise-resilient quantum communication testbeds.
one_liner: A telecom-compatible platform certifies 668-dimensional time-frequency entanglement and links it to practical high-rate QKD.
why_it_matters: It pushes high-dimensional entanglement into a scalable, measurable regime compatible with deployed fiber hardware and finite-size secure key analysis.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a scan-free characterization method for high-dimensional entanglement in the joint time-frequency domain, avoiding the cost of full tomography. Using commercial telecom components and low-jitter single-photon detectors, the authors reconstruct a state with 5.70+-0.07 ebits and 65.4+-0.4% fidelity to a maximally entangled state of local dimension 1021. They certify 668-dimensional entanglement and connect the source directly to composable finite-size entanglement-based QKD, proving an achievable secure key rate of 15.6 kB/s. The architecture is designed for continuous operation and rapid approach to asymptotic key rates.
main_result: The system demonstrates scan-free certification of 668-dimensional entanglement and supports a proven finite-size secure key rate of 15.6 kB/s.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to large-alphabet fiber QKD and other noise-resilient quantum networking schemes that need scalable high-dimensional photonic entanglement.

title: Preprocessing noise in finite-size quantum key distribution
abstract: It is known that preprocessing noise may boost quantum key distribution by expanding the range of values of tolerated noise. For BB84, adding trusted noise may allow the generation of secret keys even for qubit error rate (QBER) beyond the 11% threshold in the asymptotic regime. Here we study the effect of preprocessing noise in the finite-size regime where only a limited number of signals are exchanged between Alice and Bob. We compute tight numerical lower bounds in terms of the sandwiched Rényi entropy of order alpha, optimized via a two-step Frank-Wolfe algorithm, in the presence of a trusted flipping probability q. We find that trusted noise improves the key rate only for a finite interval of alpha, from the alpha -> 1 limit up to alpha approx 1.4. By optimizing on the value of alpha, we determine finite-size key rates for different values of the QBER, observing enhancement due to trusted noise both in asymptotic and finite-size regimes. Finally, we determine the maximum tolerable QBER as a function of the block size.
one_liner: Finite-size BB84 can benefit from trusted preprocessing noise, but only within a limited optimized Rényi-entropy regime.
why_it_matters: It sharpens when added noise is actually useful for deployable QKD, replacing asymptotic folklore with finite-block quantitative thresholds.
detailed_summary: The paper studies preprocessing noise in finite-size QKD by deriving tight numerical lower bounds based on sandwiched Rényi entropy of order alpha. The optimization is carried out with a two-step Frank-Wolfe procedure while explicitly including a trusted bit-flip probability q. The authors find that preprocessing noise improves the key rate only over a finite alpha interval, roughly from the alpha->1 limit to about 1.4, rather than uniformly. They then optimize over alpha to map finite-size key rates and the maximum tolerable QBER as a function of block size.
main_result: Trusted preprocessing noise is shown to improve finite-size secret-key rates and tolerable QBER, but only when the Rényi-order optimization falls within a restricted interval.
application_angle: The results provide practical parameter guidance for QKD systems operating with limited block lengths, where finite-size penalties dominate real deployments.

title: Iterative Decoding of Stabilizer Codes under Radiation-Induced Correlated Noise
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum computation demands extremely low logical error rates, yet superconducting qubit arrays are subject to radiation-induced correlated noise arising from cosmic-ray muon-generated quasiparticles. The quasiparticle density is unknown and time-varying, resulting in a mismatch between the true noise statistics and the priors assumed by standard decoders, and consequently, degraded logical performance. We formalize joint noise sensing and decoding using syndrome measurements by modeling the QP density as a latent variable, which governs correlation in physical errors and syndrome measurements. Starting from a variational expectation--maximization approach, we derive an iterative algorithm that alternates between QP density estimation and syndrome-based decoding under the updated noise model. Simulations of surface-code and bivariate bicycle quantum memory under radiation-induced correlated noise demonstrate a measurable reduction in logical error probability relative to baseline decoding with a uniform prior. Beyond improved decoding performance, the inferred QP density provides diagnostic information relevant to device characterization, shielding, and chip design. These results indicate that integrating physical noise estimation into decoding can mitigate correlated noise effects and relax effective error-rate requirements for fault-tolerant quantum computation.
one_liner: An iterative decoder estimates latent quasiparticle density from syndromes to suppress radiation-induced correlated errors in quantum codes.
why_it_matters: It turns decoding into a joint sensing-and-correction problem, addressing a major non-Markovian noise source in superconducting hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors model cosmic-ray-induced quasiparticle bursts as a latent variable that drives correlation in physical errors and syndrome outcomes. From a variational expectation-maximization formulation, they derive an iterative algorithm that alternates between estimating quasiparticle density and decoding under the updated noise prior. Simulations on surface codes and bivariate bicycle codes show reduced logical error probability relative to baseline decoders that assume a fixed uniform prior. The inferred latent density also provides actionable diagnostics for shielding, device characterization, and chip design.
platform_or_system: superconducting-qubit stabilizer codes under quasiparticle noise
main_result: Iterative joint noise estimation and decoding lowers logical error rates under radiation-induced correlated noise compared with standard prior-mismatched decoders.
application_angle: This approach could improve fault-tolerant performance in real superconducting processors while also informing mitigation strategies for radiation events.

title: Multi-Outcome Circuit Optimization for Enhanced Non-Gaussian State Generation
abstract: Photonic quantum computing has gained significant interest in recent years due to its potential for scaling to large numbers of qubits. A critical requirement for fault-tolerant quantum computation is the reliable generation of non-Gaussian quantum states, typically achieved using Gaussian operations and photon-number-resolving detectors. However, the probabilistic nature of quantum measurement typically results in low success rates for state preparation. Conventionally, these circuits are optimized to herald a single specific target outcome, thereby disregarding the potential utility of alternative measurement patterns generated by the same physical setup. In this work, we propose and demonstrate a multi-outcome optimization strategy that increases the overall acceptance probability by allowing a single circuit to produce useful quantum states across several measurement patterns. To evaluate this approach, we apply the framework to the generation of Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill core states, Schrodinger cat states, binomial codes, and cubic phase states using both two-mode and three-mode Gaussian circuits. We demonstrate that the success probability can be enhanced through two distinct mechanisms: first, by simultaneously targeting a diverse set of useful resource states, and second, by aggregating degenerate outcomes to maximize the production rate of a single target state.
one_liner: Photonic state-preparation circuits gain yield by optimizing for multiple useful heralding outcomes instead of a single measurement pattern.
why_it_matters: It addresses the low success probabilities of non-Gaussian resource-state generation, a central bottleneck for optical fault tolerance.
detailed_summary: This work proposes a multi-outcome circuit-design strategy for probabilistic photonic state generation with Gaussian operations and photon-number-resolving detectors. Rather than optimizing a circuit for only one heralding event, the method accepts several useful measurement patterns from the same hardware configuration. The framework is evaluated for GKP core states, Schrodinger cat states, binomial code states, and cubic phase states in two-mode and three-mode Gaussian circuits. The reported gains come from both targeting multiple distinct resource states and aggregating degenerate outcomes that realize the same target.
platform_or_system: Gaussian photonic circuits with PNR detectors
main_result: Allowing multiple accepted heralding outcomes increases the overall success probability of preparing key non-Gaussian photonic resource states.
application_angle: Higher-yield resource-state factories would reduce overhead in photonic quantum computing and make non-Gaussian state generation more practical experimentally.

title: Nonlocal Games as Cross-Platform Quantum Benchmarks: Exceeding unconditional classical bounds on trapped-ion processors
abstract: Nonlocal games provide application-level benchmarks for quantum hardware whose classical performance bounds are information-theoretic, holding against all classical strategies regardless of computational resources. We implement a 14-vertex graph coloring game, the smallest graph exhibiting a quantum-classical separation for this game type, on four trapped-ion quantum processors across three institutions. One system achieved a win rate that surpasses the classical bound with statistical significance, marking the first violation of a classical bound in a graph coloring nonlocal game on quantum hardware. The remaining systems achieved win rates comparable to the best superconducting processors evaluated on the same game, further illustrating the potential of nonlocal games as cross-architecture quantum benchmarks.
one_liner: A graph-coloring nonlocal game becomes a hardware benchmark, with one trapped-ion processor beating the unconditional classical limit.
why_it_matters: It provides an application-level benchmark whose classical bound is information-theoretic, enabling cleaner cross-platform comparisons.
detailed_summary: The paper implements a 14-vertex graph-coloring nonlocal game, the smallest instance with a quantum-classical separation of this type, on four trapped-ion processors at three institutions. Because the classical winning bound is unconditional, the benchmark does not depend on assumptions about classical computational hardness. One processor exceeds the classical limit with statistical significance, establishing the first such violation for a graph-coloring nonlocal game on quantum hardware. The remaining systems perform comparably to leading superconducting processors on the same task, supporting the use of nonlocal games as cross-architecture benchmarks.
main_result: One trapped-ion system achieved a statistically significant win rate above the unconditional classical bound in a 14-vertex graph-coloring nonlocal game.
application_angle: This gives hardware teams a rigorous, architecture-agnostic benchmark tied to observable task performance rather than isolated gate metrics.

title: Alice and Bob through a quantum mirror
abstract: A quantum mirror is a device whose optical response, that is, transmission and reflection, can be controlled by a single qubit. Here, we propose the use of quantum mirrors as nodes in quantum networks. Propagating coherent states mediate the interaction between the control qubits of each quantum mirror. This allows implementing quantum teleportation, quantum state transfer, and entanglement swapping with success probability and average fidelity exponentially approaching unity as the average photon number increases. Furthermore, we show that quantum teleportation exhibits robustness against known sources of error, such as optical path phase difference, photon loss, and reduced quantum mirror reflectivity, presenting a promising alternative towards long-distance quantum communication.
one_liner: Quantum mirrors controlled by single qubits enable coherent-state-mediated teleportation, state transfer, and entanglement swapping for networks.
why_it_matters: It proposes a network primitive with near-unity scaling in success and fidelity at higher photon numbers and resilience to realistic optical errors.
detailed_summary: The authors propose using quantum mirrors, whose reflection and transmission depend on a single control qubit, as nodes in a quantum network. In their scheme, propagating coherent states mediate interactions between mirror qubits to realize teleportation, quantum state transfer, and entanglement swapping. The protocol performance improves exponentially toward unit success probability and average fidelity as the coherent-state photon number increases. The analysis also shows robustness of teleportation against phase mismatch, photon loss, and imperfect mirror reflectivity.
platform_or_system: qubit-controlled quantum mirrors with coherent optical links
main_result: Quantum-mirror network protocols can realize teleportation and related primitives with success probability and fidelity approaching unity as photon number increases.
application_angle: If implemented, quantum mirrors could offer a robust optical-network building block for long-distance communication and distributed quantum information processing.

title: Observable-Conditioned Backaction in Dynamic Circuits: A Higher-Order Context-Conditioned Kernel for Local Dynamics
abstract: Mid-circuit measurements are essential primitives for dynamic circuits and quantum error correction, yet characterizing their induced disturbance on spectator qubits remains a central practical problem. Device-level benchmarking often compresses this disturbance into low-order proxy metrics such as $T_1$, $T_2$, readout assignment error, and pairwise crosstalk. We argue that these proxies can be operationally incomplete for multiscale dynamic circuits. We introduce a higher-order context-conditioned kernel, $Γ_{\mathrm{eff}}[Y,O] = Γ_{\mathrm{loc}}[O] + Γ_{\mathrm{proxy}}[O] + Γ_{\mathrm{rel}}[Y,O]$, where $Y$ is a global context label and $O$ a local observable. The term $Γ_{\mathrm{rel}}[Y,O]$ is a phenomenological compression ansatz isolating residual context dependence unexplained by standard proxies. To avoid impossibility issues of quantum partial-information decompositions on non-commuting algebras, the Möbius weights entering this ansatz are evaluated operationally on classical measurement outcomes. We present evidence in three steps. First, earlier GHZ-versus-clock hardware results motivate an observable-class split. Second, we present dynamical evidence using the A6 synthetic hardware harness. A6 injects a pure higher-order context dependence via a programmed conditional interaction. Because the $(C_0,C_1,C_2)$ parity context is invisible to singles and pairs by construction, standard low-order diagnostics are fundamentally blind to the source of the probe's disturbance. Third, we demonstrate coherent controllability through the A6.2 quantum-eraser experiment. Programmable MARK interactions suppress unconditional fringes while eraser-basis conditioning restores them, consistent with complementarity bounds. These results validate a context-conditioned description of backaction over proxy-only null models.
one_liner: Introduces a higher-order kernel that captures spectator-qubit backaction from mid-circuit measurements beyond standard low-order proxy metrics.
why_it_matters: It provides a more realistic framework for diagnosing dynamic-circuit disturbance that conventional T1/T2, readout, and pairwise crosstalk benchmarks can miss.
detailed_summary: The paper defines an observable- and context-conditioned backaction kernel, Γ_eff[Y,O], that augments local and proxy terms with a residual higher-order context term extracted from classical measurement outcomes. Using the A6 synthetic hardware harness, the authors engineer parity-conditioned disturbances that are invisible to all single- and two-body diagnostics by construction, demonstrating a concrete failure mode of proxy-only models. A quantum-eraser-style A6.2 experiment further shows coherent control of this context dependence through fringe suppression and conditional restoration, linking the model to operational complementarity effects.
platform_or_system: dynamic quantum circuits with synthetic hardware harness
main_result: They validate a higher-order context-conditioned model that detects measurement-induced spectator disturbance hidden from standard low-order hardware metrics.
application_angle: The framework could improve calibration and benchmarking of mid-circuit-measurement-heavy processors used for error correction and adaptive quantum algorithms.

title: Accelerated Rydberg-EIT quantum memory via shortcuts to adiabaticity
abstract: Electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) enables coherent light-matter storage, forming the basis of photonic quantum memories that are essential for scalable quantum networks and distributed quantum computing. However, accelerating the storage process violates the adiabatic condition, resulting in the excitation of the lossy intermediate state and a reduction in writing efficiency. We propose and numerically investigate a high-speed, high-fidelity quantum storage scheme by incorporating a shortcut-to-adiabaticity (STA) technique based on counter-diabatic (CD) driving. By introducing a precisely engineered auxiliary field into a conventional EIT system, our protocol significantly shortens the writing time beyond the conventional adiabatic limit while effectively suppressing the transient population of the lossy intermediate state. Furthermore, our scheme demonstrates strong flexibility in pulse design, remaining effective across different temporal profiles of both the control and signal fields. It also exhibits robustness against imperfections in the CD drive. Even with imperfect single-photon writing and non-ideal Rydberg blockade, the scheme retains clear advantages, maintaining high storage performance and overcoming the intrinsic speed-fidelity trade-off of traditional EIT protocols. These features pave the way for fast and robust quantum devices suitable for high-throughput quantum repeaters and advanced quantum information processing.
one_liner: Applies shortcut-to-adiabaticity control to Rydberg-EIT memory, enabling faster photon storage without the usual efficiency loss.
why_it_matters: Fast, robust write-in directly addresses the speed-fidelity bottleneck in quantum memories needed for repeaters and distributed photonic computing.
detailed_summary: The authors numerically study a Rydberg-EIT storage protocol augmented by a counter-diabatic auxiliary field that suppresses population of the lossy intermediate state during rapid write-in. The STA protocol preserves high storage fidelity even when the writing time is pushed beyond the normal adiabatic limit, and it remains effective across multiple control/signal pulse shapes. Simulations also indicate robustness to imperfect CD driving, nonideal single-photon input, and incomplete Rydberg blockade, showing clear performance gains over conventional EIT schemes.
main_result: The scheme achieves high-fidelity, accelerated photonic storage by using counter-diabatic control to avoid intermediate-state loss during fast EIT writing.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to high-throughput quantum repeaters and networked quantum processors that need faster memory cycles without sacrificing storage quality.

title: Inhomogeneous mass trap for dark-state polaritons in atomic media
abstract: The generation of a trapping potential for dark-state polaritons in a two-dimensional electromagnetically induced transparency system is theoretically studied. We show that such a trap can arise from a spatially inhomogeneous effective mass of the dark-state polariton. Because this mass inhomogeneity can be engineered by tuning the parameters of the control fields, the motion, spatial profile, and coherent behavior of bound dark-state polaritons can be tailored accordingly. Our results enable spatial controls of optical information and provide a possible route toward realizing Bose-Einstein condensation of dark-state polaritons in a trapping potential.
one_liner: Shows that spatially varying effective mass can trap dark-state polaritons in a 2D EIT medium without a conventional external potential.
why_it_matters: Mass-engineered trapping offers a new handle for routing, confining, and potentially condensing hybrid light-matter excitations in atomic media.
detailed_summary: This theoretical work derives a trapping mechanism for dark-state polaritons based on position-dependent effective mass in a two-dimensional EIT configuration. By tailoring the control-field parameters spatially, the authors show that one can shape the polariton dispersion and thereby localize bound polariton modes. The approach enables controllable polariton motion and spatial mode engineering, while also suggesting a route toward trapped polariton Bose-Einstein condensation in atomic systems.
platform_or_system: 2D EIT atomic medium with dark-state polaritons
main_result: They demonstrate theoretically that an inhomogeneous polariton effective mass can create a tunable trap for bound dark-state polariton states.
application_angle: The method could support spatial buffering and manipulation of optical information in atomic photonic devices and future polaritonic many-body platforms.

title: Simulating Quantum Error Correction beyond Pauli Stochastic Errors
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC), the lynchpin of fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC), is designed and validated against well-behaved Pauli stochastic error models. But in real-world deployment, QEC protocols encounter a vast array of other errors -- coherent and non-Pauli errors -- whose impacts on quantum circuits are vastly different than those of stochastic Pauli errors. The impacts of these errors on QEC and FTQC protocols have been largely unpredictable to date due to exponential classical simulation cost. Here, we show how to accurately and efficiently model the effects of coherent and non-Pauli errors on FTQC, and we study the effects of such errors on syndrome extraction for surface and bivariate bicycle codes, and on magic state cultivation. Our analysis suggests that coherent error can shift fault-tolerance thresholds, increase the space-time cost of magic state cultivation, and can increase logical error rates by an order of magnitude compared to equivalent stochastic errors. These analyses are enabled by a new technique for mapping any Markovian circuit-level error model with sufficiently small error rates onto a detector error model (DEM) for an FTQC circuit. The resulting DEM enables Monte Carlo estimation of logical error rates and noise-adapted decoding, and its parameters can be analytically related to the underlying physical noise parameters to enable approximate strong simulation.
one_liner: Presents an efficient route to simulate coherent and non-Pauli circuit noise in fault-tolerant QEC via mapped detector error models.
why_it_matters: It closes a major realism gap in QEC analysis, where thresholds and overheads can be badly misestimated by Pauli-only noise assumptions.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a technique that maps general small-rate Markovian circuit-level noise, including coherent and non-Pauli components, into detector error models suitable for Monte Carlo logical-error estimation and noise-adapted decoding. This allows efficient study of realistic noise effects in surface-code and bivariate bicycle-code syndrome extraction, as well as in magic-state cultivation. The analysis finds that coherent errors can shift thresholds, increase cultivation space-time cost, and raise logical failure rates by about an order of magnitude relative to stochastic Pauli errors with similar physical strength.
main_result: They show that realistic coherent/non-Pauli noise can be efficiently simulated and can substantially worsen logical performance compared with equivalent Pauli stochastic models.
application_angle: The method is useful for hardware-aware QEC design, decoder optimization, and resource estimation on real quantum processors where coherent control errors are unavoidable.

title: Gravitational Wave-Induced Scrambling Delay in SYK Wormhole Teleportation
abstract: Traversable wormhole teleportation in the Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model links quantum channel integrity to black hole interior dynamics, using teleportation fidelity to probe holographic scrambling. We subject the SYK boundary to a gravitational-wave (GW)-inspired periodic Floquet deformation, mimicking a leading-order metric-strain perturbation from the JT-gravity dictionary. We characterize the channel response via exact numerical time evolution with disorder averaging at $βJ = 2$. The drive produces a coherent, frequency-selective fidelity suppression, yielding four main results: (i) two amplitude regimes separated near $\varepsilon \sim J$ (perturbative sensing vs.\ strong-drive); (ii) the channel acts as a low-pass filter, most sensitive at $ω\lesssim β^{-1}$ with monotone recovery above the thermal scale; (iii) an inspiral chirp drive delays the fidelity peak by $Δt_{\rm scr}^{(\rm fid)} = +0.11\, J^{-1}$, corroborated by an out-of-time-order correlator (OTOC) diagnostic ($Δt_{\rm scr}^{(\rm OTOC)} = +0.20\, J^{-1}$), establishing a genuine scrambling delay; and (iv) the effects persist across $N \in \{10, 12, 14, 16\}$ Majorana modes, indicating no systematic finite-size suppression. These results establish that holographic teleportation channels degrade gracefully under GW-inspired boundary deformations, with direct implications for near-term quantum processor implementations of traversable wormholes.
one_liner: Studies how gravitational-wave-like periodic driving degrades SYK wormhole teleportation and delays the scrambling signature.
why_it_matters: It links channel robustness and scrambling diagnostics in a concrete driven model relevant to quantum simulations of holographic teleportation.
detailed_summary: The authors apply a GW-inspired periodic Floquet deformation to the SYK boundary Hamiltonian and track the effect on traversable wormhole teleportation fidelity under disorder-averaged exact evolution at βJ=2. They identify weak- and strong-drive regimes around ε~J, find low-pass frequency sensitivity strongest below the thermal scale, and observe a delayed fidelity peak under inspiral chirp driving. The delay is corroborated by OTOC analysis and persists across system sizes N=10 to 16 Majorana modes, indicating that the effect is not a simple finite-size artifact.
main_result: The work shows that GW-like boundary driving coherently suppresses teleportation fidelity and produces a measurable scrambling delay in SYK wormhole protocols.
application_angle: It informs analog and digital quantum hardware experiments that emulate holographic teleportation channels and need to understand drive-induced communication degradation.

title: On-chip Parametric Amplification in a Double Quantum Dots Circuit
abstract: In microwave-based quantum circuits, including double quantum dots (DQDs), superconducting qubits and spin qubits, parametric amplifiers are indispensable in achieving high-fidelity qubit readouts. Despite its importance, the application of parametric amplifiers is hampered by several challenges, such as high insertion losses, constrained tunability, and a pronounced vulnerability to magnetic fields. Here, we demonstrate an on-site single-atom parametric amplifier (SAPA) within a reconfigurable quantum circuit, which consists of a superconducting microwave cavity and two GaAs gate-defined DQDs. Leveraging the inherent nonlinearity of the DQD, a parametric gain exceeding 11 dB is achieved. This gain contributes to enhance the qubit readout, as evidenced by exceeding two times improvement in the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) when employing the DQD-based amplifier for reading out another DQD. Our work not only presents a versatile experimental platform with enhanced readout capabilities in quantum computing, but also introduces alternative choices of parametric amplifiers for a variety of microwave-based quantum circuits.
one_liner: Demonstrates an on-chip parametric amplifier based on double quantum dots with over 11 dB gain and improved qubit-readout SNR.
why_it_matters: A local amplifier compatible with reconfigurable quantum-dot circuits could reduce readout loss and broaden options beyond standard superconducting JPAs.
detailed_summary: The experiment integrates a single-atom parametric amplifier within a superconducting microwave cavity coupled to two GaAs gate-defined double quantum dots. By exploiting the intrinsic nonlinearity of one DQD, the device achieves parametric gain above 11 dB on chip. Using this DQD-based amplifier to read out the second DQD yields more than a twofold improvement in signal-to-noise ratio, demonstrating practical in-circuit amplification.
main_result: They realize an on-chip DQD parametric amplifier that delivers >11 dB gain and more than doubles the SNR for readout of another DQD.
application_angle: This provides a compact amplification strategy for spin-qubit and other microwave quantum platforms, especially where insertion loss, tunability, or magnetic-field compatibility matter.

title: Teleporting an unknown qutrit state via a 2-qudit entangled channel
abstract: We propose a quantum teleportation scheme for transmitting a single qutrit state by adopting a 2-qudit entangled state as the quantum channel. The measurement basis for Alice has been carefully and systematically constructed, which is essential for the successful implementation of the teleportation protocol. Based on Alice's measurement outcomes, we design the corresponding collective unitary transformations to be performed by Bob on an auxiliary qubit and information particle. After implementing the collective unitary transformation, Bob performs a von Neumann measurement on the auxiliary qubit. The single qutrit state is then teleported to the distant receiver Bob with a finite success probability. We obtain the achievable success probabilities of the proposed teleportation scheme for different quantum channels. The obtained results not only enrich the theory of quantum teleportation over high-dimensional entangled channels but also provide a novel and feasible approach to implementing qutrit teleportation.
one_liner: A high-dimensional teleportation protocol sends an unknown qutrit using a two-qudit entangled channel and conditional collective operations.
why_it_matters: It extends teleportation beyond qubits with explicit success probabilities, relevant to higher-capacity quantum communication primitives.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates a teleportation scheme for an unknown single-qutrit state using a bipartite two-qudit entangled resource rather than a standard qubit Bell pair. A structured measurement basis for Alice is derived, and Bob applies measurement-conditioned collective unitaries on the information particle plus an auxiliary qubit, followed by a von Neumann measurement. The authors calculate finite success probabilities for different channel states, making the protocol quantitatively analyzable for non-maximal high-dimensional entanglement. The work is mainly theoretical but technically relevant because it specifies the operations needed to realize qutrit teleportation over realistic entangled channels.
platform_or_system: qutrit over a two-qudit entangled channel
main_result: The authors present a complete probabilistic protocol for teleporting an unknown qutrit and derive its achievable success probability for different entangled channels.
application_angle: High-dimensional teleportation can increase information capacity per carrier and supports future quantum-network protocols using qutrit or qudit encodings.

title: A first-principles linear response theory for open quantum systems and its application to Orbach and direct magnetic relaxation in Ln-based coordination polymers
abstract: Single-Molecule Magnets (SMMs) exhibit slow magnetic relaxation as a result of axial magnetic anisotropy inhibiting spin-phonon transitions. In order to establish a direct link between physical observables and the microscopic theory of magnetic relaxation, we here develop and numerically implement a first-principles linear-response theory for open quantum systems that provides access to the complex a.c. magnetic susceptibility in the presence of an oscillating a.c. magnetic field. Once combined with density functional theory and multiconfigurational electronic structure simulations, this formalism is applied in a fully first-principles fashion to three cyanido-bridged Ln/Y-based coordination polymers with general formula {Ln$^{III}_x$ Y$^{III}_{1-x}$ [Co(CN)$_6$]}, where Ln = Yb (1), Tb (2), and Dy (3). The method is able to reproduce the low-temperature direct relaxation process and its field dependence, as well as the high-temperature Orbach relaxation regime for all the investigated compounds. These results demonstrate the feasibility of ab initio simulations of magnetic a.c.susceptibility in lanthanide-based SMMs and support the potential of further development of ab initio open quantum systems methods towards the completion of a magnetization dynamics theory.
one_liner: An ab initio linear-response framework predicts ac magnetic susceptibility and relaxation channels in lanthanide coordination polymers.
why_it_matters: It connects first-principles materials modeling to measurable magnetic relaxation, aiding the design of molecular quantum sensors and spin-functional materials.
detailed_summary: The authors develop and implement a first-principles linear-response theory for open quantum systems that yields complex ac magnetic susceptibility under an oscillating drive. Combined with DFT and multiconfigurational electronic-structure calculations, the method is applied to cyanido-bridged Ln/Y coordination polymers with Yb, Tb, and Dy centers. The simulations reproduce both low-temperature direct relaxation, including field dependence, and the high-temperature Orbach regime across the studied compounds. This provides a concrete microscopic route from electronic structure and spin-phonon coupling to experimentally observed magnetization dynamics.
main_result: The framework quantitatively reproduces direct and Orbach magnetic relaxation in three Ln-based coordination polymers from first principles.
application_angle: Predictive susceptibility modeling can accelerate materials discovery for molecular spin devices, magnetic sensing, and quantum-coherent lanthanide platforms.

title: Simulating Supersymmetric Quantum Mechanics Using Variational Quantum Algorithms
abstract: The study of spontaneous supersymmetry breaking (SSB) on the lattice is obstructed by a severe sign problem. Quantum computing provides a promising alternative approach. In particular, properties of supersymmetry relate SSB to the ground-state energy, which can be probed using hybrid quantum--classical algorithms such as the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE). In this work we present VQE analyses for supersymmetric quantum mechanics with various superpotentials. A key new feature is an adaptive ansatz construction algorithm that reduces the number of variational parameters within our ansätze. This lowers the resource burden on both the classical optimizer and the noisy quantum processor, thereby improving the feasibility of these calculations in the NISQ era. Additionally, we present preliminary VQE results obtained from real IBM quantum devices, highlighting accuracy, resource constraints, and computational cost, both with and without the application of error mitigation techniques.
one_liner: Adaptive VQE is used to study supersymmetric quantum mechanics, including preliminary runs on real IBM quantum processors.
why_it_matters: It shows how resource-aware variational algorithms can bring nontrivial lattice and field-theory problems closer to NISQ hardware experiments.
detailed_summary: This work applies variational quantum eigensolvers to supersymmetric quantum mechanics models whose spontaneous supersymmetry breaking can be inferred from the ground-state energy. The key technical contribution is an adaptive ansatz-construction method that reduces parameter count and therefore both optimizer and hardware overhead. The paper reports numerical studies across several superpotentials and includes early experiments on IBM devices, comparing performance with and without error mitigation. Its hardware relevance lies in mapping a difficult many-body physics target onto constrained near-term quantum processors with explicit resource considerations.
main_result: The study demonstrates that adaptive VQE can probe supersymmetric quantum mechanics with reduced ansatz complexity and feasible execution on current superconducting hardware.
application_angle: Resource-efficient variational methods broaden the class of physics simulations accessible to near-term quantum processors and help benchmark real-device capability.

title: Inverse design of a spatial demultiplexer for free-space optical communications: direct optimization over turbulence statistics
abstract: Atmospheric turbulence severely limits the coupling of received optical wavefronts into single-mode fibers in satellite-to-ground free-space optical links. Spatial demultiplexing receivers address this challenge by distributing the incoming field across a bundle of single-mode fibers whose outputs are recombined coherently, relaxing the requirements on wavefront correction. In this work, we investigate the design of such receivers from two complementary angles. We first compare the power coupling statistics achieved by several modal bases and show that the spatial support of the modes matters far more than the specific choice of basis, questioning the relevance of mode-selective approaches for this application. We then present the inverse design of a compact two-plane refractive system optimized directly over an ensemble of turbulence realizations using stochastic gradient descent, with no constraint imposed on the input modal decomposition. The optimized system significantly improves over direct coupling into the fiber bundle, approaches the performance of an ideal modal projection, and remains competitive across a broad range of turbulence conditions.
one_liner: A turbulence-aware inverse-designed spatial demultiplexer improves fiber coupling for free-space optical links across varied atmospheric conditions.
why_it_matters: It targets a practical bottleneck in satellite-to-ground optical communication by optimizing receivers directly against turbulence statistics.
detailed_summary: The paper studies receivers that distribute an aberrated free-space optical field across a bundle of single-mode fibers and then coherently recombine the outputs. It first compares different modal bases and finds that mode spatial support dominates performance more than basis selectivity, challenging standard mode-selective design assumptions. The authors then inversely design a compact two-plane refractive demultiplexer by optimizing directly over ensembles of turbulence realizations with stochastic gradient descent. The resulting device outperforms direct coupling into the fiber bundle, approaches ideal modal projection, and stays robust over a broad turbulence range.
platform_or_system: free-space optical receiver with fiber-bundle demultiplexer
main_result: A two-plane refractive demultiplexer optimized over turbulence ensembles significantly boosts coupling performance and remains competitive across varying turbulence strengths.
application_angle: Improved turbulent-channel coupling can raise link efficiency and robustness in satellite, airborne, and long-distance free-space optical communication systems.

title: A Flexible GKP-State-Embedded Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation Configuration Based on a Three-Dimensional Cluster State
abstract: The integration of diverse quantum resources and the exploitation of more degrees of freedom provide key operational flexibility for universal fault-tolerant quantum computation. In this work, we propose a flexible Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill-state-embedded fault-tolerant quantum computation architecture based on a three-dimensional cluster state constructed in polarization, frequency, and orbital angular momentum domains. Specifically, we design optical entanglement generators to produce three diverse entangled pairs, and subsequently construct a three-dimensional cluster state via a beam-splitter network with several time delays. Furthermore, we present a partially squeezed surface-GKP code to achieve fault-tolerant quantum computation and ultimately find the optimal choice of implementing the squeezing gate to give the best fault-tolerant performance (the fault-tolerant squeezing threshold is 11.5 dB). Our scheme is flexible, scalable, and experimentally feasible, providing versatile options for future optical fault-tolerant quantum computation architecture.
one_liner: A multidomain photonic 3D cluster-state architecture embeds GKP resources for flexible fault-tolerant quantum computation.
why_it_matters: It proposes an experimentally motivated optical route to scalable fault tolerance using polarization, frequency, and OAM multiplexing.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a fault-tolerant optical computing architecture based on a three-dimensional cluster state spanning polarization, frequency, and orbital angular momentum degrees of freedom. They specify entanglement generators for three distinct pair types and a beam-splitter network with time delays to assemble the 3D cluster resource. Fault tolerance is analyzed using a partially squeezed surface-GKP code, and the optimal squeezing-gate choice yields a reported threshold of 11.5 dB. The design is notable for combining multiple photonic encodings to improve flexibility and scalability in measurement-based quantum computing.
platform_or_system: multimode photonic 3D cluster state with GKP encoding
main_result: The scheme defines a scalable photonic FTQC architecture and identifies an optimal fault-tolerant squeezing threshold of 11.5 dB.
application_angle: If realized experimentally, the architecture could support multiplexed, measurement-based optical quantum processors with more practical fault-tolerance requirements.

title: Comparing optical-microwave conversion and all-microwave control schemes for a transmon qubit
abstract: We report a comparative study on transmon qubit control using (i) conventional attenuated coaxial microwave line and (ii) an optical control system using modulated laser light delivered over telecommunications optical fiber to a photodiode located at the 1K stage of a dilution cryostat. During each experiment, we performed repeated measurements of the energy relaxation and coherence times of a transmon qubit using one of the control signal delivery methods. Each measurement run spanned 20 hours of measurement time and from these datasets we observe no measurable effect on coherence of the qubit compared to random coherence fluctuations. Our results open up the possibility of large scale integration of the optical qubit control system.
one_liner: Optical-fiber delivery of qubit control signals matches conventional microwave control without measurable coherence degradation in a transmon.
why_it_matters: It supports cryogenic-compatible optical control links as a scalable alternative to dense coax wiring in large superconducting-qubit systems.
detailed_summary: This study compares transmon control through a standard attenuated coaxial microwave line versus an optical scheme in which modulated telecom laser light is sent over fiber to a photodiode mounted at the 1 K stage. Over repeated 20-hour measurement runs, the team tracks energy relaxation and coherence times under each delivery method. Within observed random coherence fluctuations, no measurable coherence penalty is found for the optical-control approach. The result is a concrete hardware benchmark for reducing cryostat wiring complexity in superconducting quantum processors.
platform_or_system: superconducting transmon with fiber-fed optical control
main_result: Fiber-delivered optical control produced transmon relaxation and coherence performance comparable to conventional all-microwave control in long-duration tests.
application_angle: Optical signal delivery could ease cryogenic wiring, thermal load, and routing constraints in larger superconducting-qubit installations.

title: Time-Multiplexed Distributed Quantum Sensing
abstract: Quantum metrology enables parameter estimation beyond classical limits by exploiting nonclassical resources such as squeezing and entanglement. In distributed quantum sensing, Heisenberg scaling has been extended from $1/N^2$ to $1/(NM)^2$ through entanglement across both particles and spatial modes, where $N$ denotes the photon number and $M$ the number of spatially distributed modes. However, the overall sensitivity has remained limited to linear scaling with the number of measurement repetitions $R$. Here, we show that exploiting entanglement across temporal modes via time-domain multiplexing enables a scaling advantage with respect to $R$. As a result, the sensitivity can asymptotically approach simultaneous Heisenberg scaling in photons, spatial modes, and repetitions, yielding an overall sensitivity approaching $Δ^2 φ\propto 1/(NMR)^2$. Using the Bogoliubov transformation formalism, we prove the optimality of the protocol within the class of Gaussian states and show that the scaling is realizable via homodyne detection and maximum-likelihood estimation. We further show that the advantage persists under optical loss and propose an experimentally feasible loop-based photonic sensing scheme. Our results open a route to incorporating time-multiplexing techniques into quantum metrology.
one_liner: A Gaussian photonic protocol uses time multiplexing to boost distributed quantum sensing toward simultaneous Heisenberg scaling in photons, sensors, and repetitions.
why_it_matters: It identifies a realistic path to turn repeated measurements into an entangled metrological resource, with loss-tolerant performance and standard homodyne-compatible readout.
detailed_summary: The paper extends distributed quantum sensing by introducing entanglement across temporal modes, not just photons and spatially separated channels. Using Bogoliubov-transform methods, it proves within Gaussian states that sensitivity can asymptotically scale as 1/(NMR)^2, effectively adding repetitions as a Heisenberg-like resource. The authors show the protocol is implementable with homodyne detection plus maximum-likelihood estimation and remains advantageous under optical loss. They also outline a loop-based photonic architecture aimed at experimental realization.
main_result: The authors show an optimal Gaussian distributed-sensing protocol whose variance asymptotically approaches Δ^2φ proportional to 1/(NMR)^2 using temporal, spatial, and photonic entanglement.
